- Disrupting the Divorce Experience. Defining Your Next Chapter. | Sadie Bjornstad | TEDxOakParkWomen: Kind: captions Language: en There is a bridge we are all familiar with. In fact, many of us have crossed this bridge. It's a beautiful bridge. It's bright and well-lit. It's paved and smooth, and everyone you know has crossed this bridge or they'll soon be crossing it after you. And it's a great experience crossing You have parties in your honor, gifts. There's people there to help you plan every step of the way. And if anything, there's an overwhelming amount of support and choices. But the best part about this bridge is where it's leading you to. It's leading you to the land of the perfect life. The land of the perfect life feels safe and secure. It's the place where you have a partner by your side, and with that maybe the ability to buy a home, have children, pets, and a whole new level of tax benefits. In the land of the perfect life, you're married. But many of us know the reality does not necessarily match this perception of perfection in our minds. And for some, it looks nothing like it. There may be daily fighting, tears, a constant tension in the house, and most of all, a sense of loss of yourself a little bit more every day. The factoid we've all heard stands true in research. Nearly 50% of all marriages end in dissolution. So, where does the 50% go when they no longer fit in the land of the perfect life and they need to leave? Well, they're not so lucky because there's another bridge that you need to cross, an exodus of the perfect life, and it looks nothing like the bridge that you crossed to get in. This bridge is old and rickety. No guardrails to hold on to, and only big enough for you to cross it alone. Far worse is what you're looking at on the other side. You picture a land that's dried up, desolate, isolated, and just overall depressing. And when you think about the people that live in that land, you wonder, "Why are they there? What happened? What's wrong with them?" But perceptions aside, in your own mind, you have broken your family, and you are a failure. Back in 2014, I was living in the land of the perfect life. I was married, a newly minted suburbanite. I have a 3-year-old beautiful 3-year-old daughter, living in a home we had spent nearly a year renovating. It was everything I had wanted on the outside. But on the inside, my marriage was crumbling. We kept reaching for milestones where we felt things had to and needed to get better, and they didn't. Around this time, I had started seeing a therapist. And we were in her office one day, and I have a memory, one of those memories where you can picture all of the details around it. Where we were sitting, what she was wearing, and the look on her face when she said, "Have you considered getting a divorce?" And I remember thinking, "How could she say that? I would never do that to my family. I could never do that to my daughter. What would work think? What would the neighbors think?" But as time went on, and as we slowly checked off every traditional method of trying to save our marriage, they we finally got to the point where I knew there were no more milestones that we could set and where everything would be okay. We were going to have to face the inevitable and cross that bridge and head to a land that neither of us wanted anything to do with. And I felt scared and ashamed. But my journey took a few turns that I did not anticipate, and it led me to a land that actually looked nothing like that dried up sad place that I had envisioned in my mind. The first curve in the road I traced back to a day right around when all of this was happening, and I was walking through the airport quickly at when I was on a work trip, and as I was walking past the Hudson News, something caught my eye. And I stopped, and I walked up and picked up the cover of an uh uh the uh cover of the Us Weekly magazine. And on the cover was Gwyneth Paltrow and Chris Martin with a big tear down the middle. And I remember thinking, "Oh, Gwyneth is going through this, too. Like, we are going to go through this together." And bigger yet, well, how are I can't believe they have to leave the land of the perfect life, and how are they doing it? And while this cover and this story basically made fun of them for coining a new cheesy term for divorce, I was fascinated. And so then I dove into the Goop website with a vengeance. I read about their desire to keep their family as a unit while they themselves would no longer be a couple. And before this, all I knew of was of divorce was that you each get a lawyer, you start duking it out, and everything is miserable and terrible for the rest of your life. That article served as a guidepost for us to navigate through our own experience in a more positive way. And it really fundamentally changed the way that we approached our situation. The second area came around figuring out where to live and figuring it out fast. Our house sold the day it went on the market, and the buyers wanted an early close. And I was like, "Where am I going to live?" So, I started looking through places, and it really sent me to an all-time low. The places that were in my single mom price range was pretty gut-wrenching to see after putting all the blood, sweat, and tears into the renovation of our home. And I had moved into our home feeling that was the place that my daughter was going to grow up in. On the day I was signing a lease for an apartment that I didn't even like, a community email came into my inbox. And as I searched through, there was a house in it that needed renters. It was beautiful, newly renovated, much too big for my daughter and I. But an idea struck. I approached a friend of mine who was also recently divorced with a 3-year-old daughter. And I said something like, "Look at this amazing house. Could you imagine living here?" And I mean, this could be ridiculous, but what if we lived there together? I mean, who would do that? Uh but as we talked through it, the idea became less and less ridiculous to us. And we decided, "Well, hey, what if we try this for a year?" And that was 3 years ago. And the last 3 years have been the most rewarding, fun, and meaningful years of my life. But I wanted to understand how and why it was so different from that land I had pictured in my mind. And I knew it was more than just having a great living situation and being able to co-parent successfully. It was something that I felt differently about. So, after some soul-searching, I figured out that the reason that it was so different is because I no longer equated the end of my marriage with failure. I had somewhere along the line made an unconscious decision to flip what I thought was a failure into an opportunity to create another perfect life. But I wanted to know more because all I saw around me were women and couples that were struggling with that failure and that shame. So, I started doing research, and I've spent the last 3 years reading, talking to women in the community and across the country, and I found that there is a common theme that women who have been able to navigate through in a more positive and solutions-oriented oriented mindset share. And the common theme is this. You focus on the things that you can control, and you let go of the things that you can't. You are the one who is in charge of defining your experience. But how do you begin to define an experience that you want nothing to do with and you know nothing about? Where do you even start? Well, these days when you're not sure how to do something and you need some answers, what do you turn to? Google. You got it. But in some cases, and especially with this case, it's not as easy as typing in a couple search terms and finding some clarity on the other end. In many areas of our lives, we turn to role models and mentors. We do this in sports, in our jobs and careers, in school. So, why not in divorce? The first thing that you need to do is figure out what is important to you, and then you can find someone who shares some of those same values. And you your experience is very unique, and you may think, "I don't think I know anyone who has gone through a divorce that looks like they share any of my same values." But sometimes when you start looking and listening for things and talking, you find you have more in common with people that are out there. And even if you can't find one person, you sometimes are able to look at a lot of different situations, and whether you know them personally or you see them virtually, and you take a little bit of good from everyone's situation, and you mold it together, and you define an experience of what looks like success for you. It might be looking at a woman who is not working, and she went back to school and is now has a job and is earning significant income. Or maybe it's a woman that was left high and dry, but she puts a smile on her face and is able to co-parent successfully. You figure out what you value, you find someone who shares those same values, and emulate them. And maybe one day you can become a mentor for someone else. The next area is around opening our minds to the trends and changes that are happening in the world around us. There are so many things in our lives that are changing so fast that it blows our minds. Some things are so different than they were 10 years ago or 2 years ago, even 2 months ago. But divorce is definitely not one of those things. The stigma around what divorce is like and how terrible it is is almost ingrained into our brains. This stigma is a primary contributor to that failure mindset. The world and how we live in it is changing fast, and women have more education, opportunity, and information at our fingertips. We can tap into this technology and the trends to help us build resources, simplify our lives, and connect to others like us. How can divorce get with the times? Well, one example is looking at how and where we live. And while I fell into the opportunity to live with a friend and rent a home, co-living is actually become is a rising trend in the United States and across the world. In some countries like Japan, it's an established way to live for some singles. Startups are jumping on this notion that they can create better living through convenience and community and are building communal homes. Traditional housing is built on the antiquated notion of individuality and the suburban American dream. But what if we look at housing that is designed around the notion and our fundamental needs for community and social interaction? The communal homes can help with splitting expenses, maintaining a lifestyle, and providing that community. All three things are so important when we are going through tough times in our lives. Another example of getting with the times is that we are living in what they call the gig economy. Side hustles are becoming the new norm with bringing opportunity for additional income at our fingertips. Driving, blogging, renting things out, selling things on Etsy. They're all ways that you can shape things around your lifestyle and things you enjoy doing and that you're good at. These are just two examples of some things that are available to us now that weren't available to us 20 years ago. Being able to just simply know that they exist can help us shape a different definition of how we approach our next chapter. The third area is around rebuilding our community. And what I mean by community is simply your friend circle and the people that you surround yourself with. Because rest assured, this will get shaken up if you go through a divorce, and it's something you just have to be prepared for. I remember a time shortly after our divorce when I sent a text message to a group of moms to have a playdate, and no one responded, but I didn't think anything of it. And a few days later, I sent another text, and again, no response. And I had this silly aha moment where I thought, "My phone has been so slow and wonky. I need to restart it." And sure enough, when I turned my phone back on, the text messages did not come flooding in as I was expecting them to. And I felt really sorry for myself and I spent a couple days shedding some tears, and I decided that it was high time to change my own narrative, and it was time to reshape my circle. I was going to focus on the relationships that I felt good about and not focus on the ones that made me feel bad or brought in negativity. And I'd reconnect with friends I'd lost touch with, and then I was going to dig deep and make some new friends. And I like to I liken it to the wardrobe refresh of the early 2000s to 2017. Cuz you may need to go in there and dig through that closet and pull out all those pairs of Express work pants that were the foundation of your wardrobe, but you need to hold on to that one pair of pants from Theory that fits you really well. And then, slowly and deliberately, you add back in new pieces, pieces of high quality that fit you well and make you feel confident in who you are. Reshaping your community is a really cool area because you have some control here. You're the one in charge, and you're in the driver's seat of who you surround yourself with. But it's not a passive area. It might take you talking to more people than you normally would and being a little bit vulnerable, asking the moms at school for coffee, or trying to host a party and then praying that people show up. But it can be a fundamental change in how you live your life and how you feel. Simon Sinek has said, "Before we can build the world we want to live in, we have to imagine it." So now, I want you to imagine it. Imagine this. There is no bridge that the 50% has to cross to leave the land of the perfect Rather, we stay in the land of the perfect life, and we redefine how we live in it. We do this by following positive role models, tapping into the trends and technology around us to help us maintain a quality of life, and reshaping the neighbors who surround us to provide more support and meaning in our lives. I believe that when you change your perception, you can change your experience. And this is one experience that is ready for a change. Thank you.

With the taxable brokerage, the kids get the date-of-death balance tax-free, yes, but with Roth they get that plus an extra decade of tax-free growth.

- Boat Lemon Law Explained: Know Your Legal Rights as a Watercraft Owner: Kind: captions Language: en Boat lemon laws are legal protections for consumers who purchase defective watercraft. Though not as widely recognized as autolemon laws, they are emerging to shield buyers from ongoing boat issues. These laws often mirror auto protections and cover new or warrantied boats like motorboats, sailboats, jet skis, and cabin cruisers. In states without specific boat limit laws like California, buyers can turn to the Magnus and Moss Warranty Act, state warranties, or the Uniform Commercial Code, UCCC. The Magnus and Moss Act ensures that manufacturers repair defects within a reasonable time frame or offer a refund, replacement, or compensation. Qualifying defects must significantly affect the boat's safety, function, or value, and include issues like engine failure, electrical faults, leaks, or steering malfunctions. Documentation is critical. Buyers should keep records of repairs, communications, warranties, and photographic proof. Manufacturers must be given a reasonable number of repair attempts, typically three, four, or if the boat is out of service for 30 plus days. Legal claims can result in a buyback, replacement, or reimbursement of expenses. Used boats have fewer protections, but may still qualify if under warranty, CPO certified, or sold with undisclosed faults. Some states, including Florida, Connecticut, Ohio, and Texas provide boat specific lemon law coverage. Buyers are advised to consult with specialized lemon law attorneys, many of whom offer free consultations and work on contingency. Understanding these rights helps owners secure resolutions and return to boating without stress. If you have a lemon, please call Julian McMillan at McMillan Law Group at 61979-59-430. That's 61979-59430 or email us at julenthe rate of mcmillanawgroup.com for your free case evaluation.

- Before You Buy That Yacht: 6 Legal Traps Florida Boat Owners Must Avoid: Kind: captions Language: en-US You’re standing on the deck of a 65 foot Sunseeker&nbsp; docked in Fort Lauderdale. The teak is polished,&nbsp;&nbsp; the engines are humming, and the broker&nbsp; is smiling, ready to take your signature&nbsp;&nbsp; and wire transfer. But behind the champagne&nbsp; and sales pitch, there could be hidden liens,&nbsp;&nbsp; registration nightmares, or tax traps that’ll sink&nbsp; your investment before you ever leave the marina.&nbsp; I’m Santiago Cueto, a board certified&nbsp; international business attorney based in Miami,&nbsp;&nbsp; and today I’m giving you the legal checklist&nbsp; every Florida yacht buyer must know, so your&nbsp;&nbsp; dream boat doesn’t turn into a legal shipwreck. Now let’s dive into the six power plays that will&nbsp;&nbsp; make your yacht purchase in Florida&nbsp; smooth, secure, and storm proof.&nbsp; Power Play #1: Run a Full&nbsp; Lien & Title Check, Twice&nbsp; Never assume clean title. Florida yachts may be&nbsp; registered under state law, federal documentation,&nbsp;&nbsp; or offshore structures, each with its own&nbsp; records. Run a UCC search through the Florida&nbsp;&nbsp; Secured Transaction Registry and check the U.S.&nbsp; Coast Guard’s National Vessel Documentation&nbsp;&nbsp; Center. If a lien shows up, don’t close until&nbsp; you have a signed, notarized release in hand.&nbsp; Power Play #2: Know the Rules of&nbsp; Registration vs. Documentation&nbsp; In Florida, boats must be either registered&nbsp; with the state or federally documented,&nbsp;&nbsp; not both. Documented yachts are better for&nbsp; international use and charter operations, but&nbsp;&nbsp; they still trigger Florida’s tax and compliance&nbsp; requirements. Misunderstanding this can lead to&nbsp;&nbsp; double-taxation or even seizure at port. Power Play #3: Structure Around&nbsp;&nbsp; the Sales Tax, Legally Florida caps yacht sales tax at&nbsp;&nbsp; $18,000, a huge savings on high-value vessels.&nbsp; But you only get that cap if the transaction is&nbsp;&nbsp; structured properly. Use a Florida based closing&nbsp; agent, file the required DR-42A tax form, and&nbsp;&nbsp; if relocating the vessel within 90 days, pursue&nbsp; the out-of-state exemption with ironclad proof.&nbsp; Power Play #4: Demand a&nbsp; Certified Survey and Sea Trial&nbsp; This isn’t just due diligence, it’s your&nbsp; defense if the engine fails two weeks&nbsp;&nbsp; after purchase. Hire a licensed marine surveyor,&nbsp; conduct a full mechanical and hull inspection,&nbsp;&nbsp; and take the vessel for a sea trial. If problems&nbsp; arise, you can negotiate repairs or walk away,&nbsp;&nbsp; before it becomes your problem. Power Play #5:&nbsp;&nbsp; Upgrade the Standard Purchase Contract Those broker-supplied contracts? Designed to&nbsp;&nbsp; protect them, not you. Have an attorney draft&nbsp; or review a custom agreement that includes:&nbsp; Clear survey contingencies Escrow protections&nbsp; Lien-free delivery guarantees Risk of loss clauses&nbsp; Refund rights if conditions aren’t met And lock jurisdiction in Florida,&nbsp;&nbsp; you don’t want to fight a Cayman&nbsp; lawsuit over a Fort Lauderdale boat.&nbsp; Power Play #6: Buy Through an LLC or Trust Yachts are assets, and liabilities. Structure&nbsp;&nbsp; ownership through a Florida LLC or irrevocable&nbsp; trust to protect personal wealth from maritime&nbsp;&nbsp; injury claims or business disputes. If&nbsp; you’re planning to charter the yacht,&nbsp;&nbsp; commercial ownership also unlocks&nbsp; additional tax and insurance benefits.&nbsp; Make the Yacht Yours the Right Way Buying a yacht in Florida isn’t just a&nbsp;&nbsp; luxury move, it’s a legal transaction that demands&nbsp; precision. Pull the right records, plan the taxes,&nbsp;&nbsp; inspect thoroughly, and paper it professionally.&nbsp; With these six power plays, your yacht won’t just&nbsp;&nbsp; turn heads, it’ll be legally watertight. If you found this guide helpful,&nbsp;&nbsp; smash that Like button, subscribe for&nbsp; more Florida legal and business insights

In fractional aviation, it works because it is a procedural industry. There are three pillars. First, the operator, a professional manages it. Second, [music] procedures, standards, checklists, maintenance, planning. Third, settlements. strict rules that do not depend on mood and that is why people can have shares and it can work even when they do not know each other. Yachts are different. It's about how the salon should look, how the cabin should smell, what toys, what standard. And that's why classic yacht co-ownership breaks down faster because a share often creates a need for influence. and influence breeds conflicts. In practice, it looks like this. Someone has a 20% share and suddenly wants 20% of the decisions about everything. Another has 30% and wants 30% of the decisions. And before the yacht even starts working in charter, politics begins. Interior, equipment, crew, homeport, standards, budget. This is a beautiful recipe for the yacht not to be a business, but an emotional project. And here a completely different philosophy comes into play. The yachti idea is different. You do not buy the right to arrange a yacht. You buy a minority stake in the charter business. And that changes everything because in such a model there are many more owners. So no one has the power to argue over every detail. The standard is maintained by a professional operator and you are a shareholder in the outcome. Why did it rarely work with small amounts before? Because without tokenization, co-ownership had to be limited to a few people. And a few people means companies, contracts, registers, lawyers, accounting, signatures, share transfers, then manual settlements. This does not scale to hundreds or thousands of small shareholders. Paper and administration have their limits. And blockchain does something that sounds boring, but is a revolution. It can scale ownership. Blockchain [music] provides a simple mechanism. Someone who has €1,000 euros can participate in the charter business, not as a ship owner, as a minority shareholder. In this model, the hard cap is also important. There are exactly as many tokens as corresponds to 100% of the value of the yacht. After the entire amount is sold, tokenization is closed without any additional printing. There is no risk that the profit will be divided into more shares. There is no delusion. This builds trust because everyone knows the total amount. Now we are entering the heart of the system. The smart contract has two key roles. The first role, it monitors the shares. You deposit, you receive tokens. The tokens reflect your share. This is not Excel. This is not someone forgot to add something. This is the mechanics of ownership. The second part of the same role is process security. The logic of first we collect the full amount then we buy the yacht. In other words, only collecting 100% allows us to proceed to finalization. This solves the classic problem of crowdfunding projects. What if we run out before the end? Here the principle is strict and mechanical. And now mega important. Because although the topic may seem bureaucratic at first glance, in reality, the choice of flag determines how much we pay, where we can sail, and how safely we can use the yacht. Let's start with the question every yacht owner asks. [music] Where is it best to register a yacht? In Poland, Malta, or perhaps the Cayman Islands. Let's start from the beginning. Every yacht in the world must have a flag. That is the flag of the country where it is registered. But the flag is not just a symbol on the stern. It's much more than that. It's the yacht's legal identity. It's the flag that determines which law applies on board. In practice, who is responsible for [music] the vessel, its crew, and its taxes. A yacht's deck is considered the territory of the flag state. [music] So if your yacht sails under the Polish flag, it's as if you were in Poland. The key question is whether the yacht will be used privately or registered as a commercial vessel, meaning it will be formally available for charter. This choice has a fundamental impact on taxation. A private [music] yacht is used exclusively by the owner and their guests. It cannot be rented out or operate commercially. It's the simplest though most expensive model. You pay VAT once and you're set for years. But there's also a second option, commercial [music] registration. In this case, the yacht operates like a commercial vessel. It is formally designated for charters or for carrying passengers [music] for a fee. And this is where the tax magic begins. A commercially registered yacht can be exempt from value added tax on purchase and may also benefit from deductions on fuel, parts, and maintenance services. These savings can reach hundreds [music] of thousands of euros. For example, in Malta, VAT can be reduced from over 19% to as little as 5%. For a yacht worth €1.5 [music] million, that difference amounts to around €200,000. It's no surprise then that many owners try to take advantage of this model. But remember, such a structure only works if the yacht truly operates as a charter business. And if it's only a [music] sham for the tax authorities, the risk is enormous. Why do such tax benefits exist in the first place? Because maritime law was created not for luxury, but for the economy. Commercial ships, fies, and fishing vessels have enjoyed tax exemptions for decades. Their strategic sectors of transport and food supply. If they had to pay vati [music] on everything, they would simply become unprofitable. That's why the European Union in article 148 of the VAT directive [music] clearly states vessels used for the transport of passengers for remuneration, [music] for fishing or for commercial navigation are exempt from VAT. The problem is that luxury yachts are also technically seagoing vessels. So in theory [music] they can fit within these regulations. And this is where interpretation comes into play. Whether a given yacht is truly working or merely pretending to be. Today we'll take a closer look at how yacht owners and tax authorities engage in a constant game of cat and mouse searching for the most optimal tax solutions. This is the main battleground where on one side owners seek to minimize costs and on the other tax authorities strive to prove that a yacht is a luxury item not a means of transport. An example of this dynamic relationship is France which for many years offered the so-called French commercial exemption. Commercial yachts could be completely exempt from VAT even if they operated solely in the Mediterranean, a setup that was extremely advantageous for luxury yacht owners. However, in 2010, the European Commission deemed this system too liberal and introduced changes. From that moment on, the exemption has applied only to vessels that actually sail on the high seas, which has significantly limited its use. Another example of a creative approach to tax optimization is Malta, which developed an innovative leasing structure. In this system, Viet was calculated on a flat rate basis with a simple assumption. The larger the yacht, the more it sails outside the EU and therefore the less Viat it pays. However, the European Union considered this approach too creative. And since 2019, Malta has been required to demand proof of voyages outside the EU. Yacht owners must now provide voyage logs, AIS records, and port documents to prove their activity outside the territory of the European Union. Without real data, there's no chance of a VAT exemption, proving that the game between yacht owners and tax authorities is very much alive with no sign of ending anytime soon. Let's now move on to the most popular flags chosen by yacht owners. Among them, Poland, Malta, Cyprus, the Cayman Islands, and the Marshall Islands reign supreme, each offering its own unique advantages. The Polish flag has become a real hit, not only because of the charm of the Masurian lakes, but mainly because Poland offers the cheapest and simplest yacht registration process in all of Europe. The online registration process is intuitive and easily accessible, attracting many yacht owners from across the European Union. The online registration process is intuitive and accessible, attracting many yacht owners from across the European Union. Meanwhile, Malta and Cyprus are favorite choices for tax reasons. They offer the most effective options for VAT optimization. These countries offer advanced structures that allow for a significant reduction in tax burdens, a key factor for many investors. The Cayman Islands and the Marshall Islands represent the offshore level, offering full confidentiality, zero tax, and an export status for the yacht. This solution is chosen by owners who purchase a new yacht in Europe but register it outside the EU completely avoiding that. The Cayman flag, a light blue en featuring the Union Jack and the Cayman Islands coat of arms symbolizes their status as a British overseas territory. In practice, yachts are divided into two worlds in terms of VRT status, VAT paid and temporary import fat. The first is VAT paid, meaning the tax has been fully paid, allowing the yacht to sail freely throughout the entire European Union without any restrictions. It's the safest and most convenient form of ownership, offering peace of mind and freedom from additional formalities. VAT can also be deducted if the yacht is used for legitimate charter business activities. The second is temporary import, which applies to yachts registered outside the EU. It allows the vessel to stay within the union for up to 18 months without paying VAT. After that period, the yacht must leave the EU even for just one day out to reset the clock. This approach is popular among owners from the USA, Switzerland, and the Middle East. However, a word of caution. If such a yacht stays in Europe for too long, VAT will be charged on its entire value, which can result in enormous costs. That's why when buying a used yacht, it's always worth asking, does this yacht have VAT paid status? In most EU countries, purchasing a new yacht involves paying vati, the rate of which varies depending on the country. For example, in France, the standard rate is 20%, in Italy, 22%, and in Spain, 21%, illustrating how important the choice of registration country really is. Choosing the right registration jurisdiction affects both the financial burden and the obligations that the yacht owner must meet. For vessels imported from outside the EU, a key issue is the obligation to pay customs duties and VAT upon import. The customs duty on yachts in the European Union is typically around 1.7%, although it may vary depending on the vessel's specifications and country of origin. Determining the place of supply of services is one of the most important elements of the VAT taxation model. Determining the place of service provision is one of the most important elements of the VAT taxation model. It can decide whether taxation applies for example in Poland or outside the country which may among other things require VAT registration in another state. Now let's talk about the risks, consequences and strategic decisions involved in choosing a flag state. If the authorities determine that the commercial registration was a sham, the consequences are immediate and very serious. VAT is then charged retroactively and the yacht is often seized as collateral for the outstanding payment. But that's not all. If the yacht is registered under a company engaged in another line of business, such as construction or manufacturing, that company becomes liable with all of its assets. The tax authorities can seize its bank accounts, contracts, and operating funds, potentially paralyzing the entire business. That's why experienced yacht owners set up dedicated companies that handle only the yacht. These are known as SPVS, specialurpose vehicles. It's a small detail, but one that can protect the owner's entire wealth, shielding them from unforeseen legal and financial consequences. Choosing a flag is not only about taxes. It also determines labor regulations and crew costs, which have a major impact on the overall budget. Every crude yacht, even a private one, must comply with the labor laws of its flag state and within the maritime labor convention, MLC. Some flags, especially the so-called flags of convenience, allow the employment of lowerc cost crews while bypassing certain European labor restrictions. Others, like France or Italy, require full compliance with regulations, insurance coverage, and minimum wage standards, which significantly increase operating costs. For large yachts, this difference can amount to several hundred,000 per year, a significant figure in the owner's budget. That's why the flag affects not only taxation but also the day-to-day operating costs of the vessel directly influencing its profitability. But that's a topic for another episode. There's truly a lot to discuss and the details are remarkably complex. To sum up, yacht registration is a strategic decision that requires careful thought and thorough analysis. It's not just about which flag flies from the stern. It's about the laws, taxes, and obligations that come with it. Because a yacht is not only a symbol of luxury, it's also a serious legal and financial commitment that must be carefully considered. - She Didn’t Argue During Divorce — Later Seen Boarding a Billionaire’s Mega-Yacht: Kind: captions Language: en She sat in the divorce hearing as silent as a statue. While her husband Julian smirked and divided their assets, Allar Vance said nothing. She didn't fight. She didn't cry. She didn't argue for a single dollar more. He thought she was broken. A beautiful empty shell he was finally discarding. He left the courtroom with his young mistress laughing. Six months later, that same laugh died in his throat when he saw the paparazzi photos, ara stepping onto the world's most exclusive mega yacht in Monaco, greeted at the top, not by a new lover, but by the one man who could destroy him completely. The 48th floor conference room at Jacobson Low and Stone was a tomb of imported marble stifling gray upholstery and the smell of old money. The air recycled and chilled to a precise 68° felt too thin to breathe. It was a room built for the quiet, bloodless execution of lives, marriages, and fortunes. Across a vast, reflective mahogany table, Julian Thorne watched his wife, his soon-to-be ex-wife, Elara Vance. She was still he had to give her that an aesthetic triumph. She wore a simple sleeveless sheath dress in a shade of cream that probably had a name like eggshell or oyster. It was designed to signal quiet expense, a taste he had cultivated in her. Her dark, almost black hair was pulled back into a shiny so severe it looked painful, drawing her high cheekbones into sharp relief. But it was her eyes that unnerved him. They were a clear crystalline blue, and they were fixed on the $5,000 floral arrangement in the center of the table, as if it held the secrets of the universe. They were, he realized, completely blank. There was no rage, no sorrow, no anything. As you can see, his lawyer, Robert Jacobson, a man whose tailored pinstripes and reptilian smile cost $2,000 an hour, purrred, "Mr. Thorne, has been exceptionally, one might even say, profoundly generous." Julian allowed a small, indulgent smile to touch his lips. He was being generous. He was giving her the downtown condo, a property he'd bought for her as a hobby studio a decade ago and had literally forgotten he owned. He was giving her a lump sum, a severance package, really. Seven figures, Robert had called it, as if it were a grand gesture. To Julian, whose net worth was a number so large it was abstract, it was a rounding error. It was what he'd spent on Khloe's birthday weekend. He had been braced for war. When he'd first told her 6 months ago, it had been a calculated ambush. He'd come home from a business trip to St. Barts with Kloe and simply informed Aara that he was in love and the marriage was over. He had expected the full oporadic display, the screaming, the accusations, the tears, the how could you after all I've given up for you speech. He had his responses ready. He had his checkbook open. Instead, Ara had looked up from the book she was reading. She had processed his words, and after a silence that stretched for an agonizing 10 seconds, she had simply said, "I see." She had then closed her book, placed it on the side table, and walked upstairs. By the next morning, she and a few suitcases were gone. No mess, no drama, just a vacuum. Her silence had been anticlimactic. It had cheated him of his victory of the moment where he got to be the magnanimous regretful conqueror. Now in this sterile conference room, her silence was just irritating. Ara Robert prompted the silk in his voice fraying into impatience. These terms are, as I've stated, non-negotiable, but we do require your signature to move forward. Aar's gaze finally slowly lifted from the flowers. It drifted past Robert, past her own lawyer, a mousy middle-aged woman named Sarah, who looked thoroughly out of her depth and landed on Julian. His breath caught. He was prepared for hatred. He was prepared for tears. He was not prepared for the polite, impersonal emptiness he saw. It was the look you'd give to a stranger on a crowded subway. A fraction of a second of acknowledgement before looking away. She wasn't looking at her husband. She was looking at an obstacle. "I accept the terms," she said, her voice, clear and low, did not tremor. It was the first time he'd heard it in months, and it was the voice of a stranger. Julian almost flinched. He'd wanted her to fight. He'd wanted to win that fight. He wanted to tell her, "Elara, be reasonable. This is what's best. I need to be happy." Her quiet immediate capitulation felt like a trick. It was unnatural. It only confirmed his long-held private diagnosis. She was weak, fragile. She had built her entire existence on the scaffolding of his life. And now that he was kicking it out from under her, she was simply collapsing. Her lawyer, Sarah, looked visibly relieved, as if she'd expected her client to be eviscerated. She fumbled with the thick stack of documents and slid them across the polished wood. "Mrs. Thorne, Ara," Sarah began. "Are you sure you don't want to?" "I am sure," Aara said, cutting her off. "Not unkindly, but with a finality that made even Robert Jacobson sit up a little straighter." Ara reached into her simple unbranded leather handbag. She ignored the row of gleaming silver Mont Blanc pens laid out like surgical tools. She pulled out a cheap blue plastic bick, the kind you buy in a pack of 10. She clicked it. The sound of sharp snick was obscenely loud in the quiet room. She didn't read the documents. She simply turned to the last page. She signed her name. All Vance, not Thorne. She had already reclaimed her name. She kept the pen. She stood. She nodded once a quick dismissive gesture to her own lawyer. She didn't look at Julian. She didn't look at Robert. She picked up her bag and walked to the enormous oak doors. "Elara, wait," Julian said, standing up. It was an involuntary impulse. She paused her hand on the brass handle, but she did not turn around. "What for, Julian?" "You're free. Isn't that what you wanted?" She opened the door and was gone. The whoosh of the door closer was the only sound. Robert Jacobson let out a long, slow breath. Well, that was the easiest high-profile divorce of my career. That lumpsum, Julian, she'll be back in 6 months when it's gone. She doesn't know how to manage. She'll be fine, Julian said, sinking back into his chair. He loosened his Hermes tie. She's resilient. He was already pulling out his phone. He sent a text to Khloe who was waiting at Perce for a celebratory lunch. It's done. Order the 82 Patris. He felt a profound giddy sense of relief. He was free. He was on his way to Monaco next month to close the Apex deal, the one that would make him a legend. He was on his way to a new life with a 24year-old who adored him. He was Julian Thorne, and he had just won. So why, as he rode the elevator down in a cocoon of mirrored steel, did the image of her blank eyes and that cheap plastic pen make him feel a sudden unwelcome chill? He shook it off. It was just the echo of a life now finished. All Vance did not take a cab to a friend's house. She did not go to a bar for a stiff drink. She did not go to the airport to find herself in Italy. She took the subway anonymous face in the afternoon crowd and went to the downtown condo, the one Julian had so generously allowed her to keep, not knowing it had always been legally and emotionally her escape pod. The condo was the antithesis of the opulent, suffocating penthouse she had shared with Julian. The penthouse was all gold leaf dark woods and heavy silks a museum Julian had built to himself. The condo was a clean white box. It was a space of hard surfaces, white walls, and floor to allse ceiling windows that looked out over the glittering impersonal grid of the city. The main living area was dominated not by a sofa, but by a customuilt workstation, three 4K monitors, a server tower humming quietly in a ventilated cabinet, and a keyboard that clicked with the satisfying mechanical precision of a weapon. She walked to the console table by the door and placed her keys in a small white porcelain bowl. She took off her shoes. She went to the minimalist kitchen, pulled a bottle of sparkling water from the fridge, and drank it her hand as steady as a surgeon's. She had not been silent for the last 6 months. She had been meticulous. Julian Thorne had made the most common mistake of powerful men. He had assumed a lack of ego meant a lack of intellect. He had met Aara at a modern art gallery opening. He saw a stunning, quiet art history major. He saw a woman who was content to be a beautiful placid accessory. He did not see the other ar. The arara who had graduated from MIT at 19 with a dual degree in mathematics and quantitative economics. The ara who before she'd even turned 21 was the most sought-after analyst at a secretive hedge fund so exclusive it didn't have a name on its building. She had built the predictive algorithms that saw the 2008 crash coming 6 months before anyone else. She had walked away from it all for Julian. He had found her intelligence intimidating. I don't want to come home and talk about derivatives, he'd laughed early in their relationship. I want to come home and just be. I want a partner, a wife. So, she had become one. She had packed her brilliant, restless mind away, hiding it behind a mask of placid elegance. She learned to plan his gala manage his three households and listen politely to his monologues about killer instincts. She'd believed for a brief foolish time that it was a fair trade for love. The day she found a single blond rooted hair extension and a Cardier earring in his travel bag was the day the trade was cancelled. But it wasn't the infidelity that had sealed Julian's fate. It was what she found three days later. She was in his home office, a room he called the sanctuary, looking for a tax document. Julian, for all his genius, was organizationally a slob. He threw everything into drawers. Tucked inside a vintage leatherbound copy of The Art of War, a book she knew he'd never read, was a small matte black encrypted hard drive. He had used the same password he used for everything the stock ticker of his first big win, APX1. It took less than four minutes to bypass the basic encryption. It took her the rest of the night to understand what she was looking at. Julian wasn't just a shark. He was a highlevel criminal. He was running a sophisticated multi-year securities fraud. He wasn't just cheating on her. He was cheating everyone. He was using a web of Shell Corporations, Signis Ventures, Nautilus Group, to artificially inflate the value of his private equity holdings before dumping them on his own investors. It was a classic pump and dump, but on a scale that was breathtaking in its audacity. The Apex deal, the one he was moving to Monaco for, was to be his masterpiece, a final massive score before he exited, leaving his partners and investors holding a multi-billion dollar bag of worthless stock. Aar sat on the floor of his office until the sun came up the glow of the laptop illuminating her face. She didn't feel anger. She didn't feel betrayal. She felt cold. It was the clean, pure, exhilarating cold of a complex equation finally beautifully balancing. The trophy wife died on that floor. The quant was reborn. She copied the drive. She spent the next two months not in spas or at lunchons, but in this condo. She traced the money. She cross-referenced every transaction. She taught herself to code in three new languages to follow the data trails through untraceable servers in the Caymans in Eastern Europe. She built a case file, an ironclad, datadriven, mathematically certain case that would not just put Julian in prison. It would dismantle his entire empire. It was then she realized why she couldn't argue in the divorce. Arguing meant attachment. Fighting for his generous settlement meant accepting his blood money. She wanted nothing from him. Her silence wasn't weakness. It was a quarantine. Now standing in her kitchen, she finished her water. She walked into her bedroom. On her dresser, next to a single framed photograph was a satellite phone. The photo was of her 20 years old in a graduation cap and gown standing next to a rugged silver-haired man. She picked up the phone and dialed the single number in its memory. A voice answered on the first ring. Cold, clean, and absolute. Outside, the sun was setting on Monaco. But in the command center of the Olympus, Aara Vance's world was just beginning. Ara Vance's story teaches us a powerful lesson. Never mistake silence for weakness. Julian saw his wife as a beautiful decoration, and his arrogance was his downfall. He couldn't see the brilliant mind working behind those quiet eyes, a mind that wasn't plotting revenge, but was calculating his end. She didn't fight him for his scraps because she was already planning to buy his entire company. She didn't argue over the divorce settlement because she was positioning herself to become the billionaire, not just marry one. What did you think of Allar's ultimate power move?
