- The Cost of Freedom 01- Money: What It Actually Cost our Family to Sail Around the World for 8 Years: Kind: captions Language: en Well, with the Rena's busy work schedule, we can't seem to find a mutually agreeable time put this put together. And next week, she's up north again. So, I think we'll plow on anyway, just you and me, because there's a lot of material I want to get through. Today's subject is on a subject I wanted to tackle probably for a while. After the recent coverage with the BBC, Telegraph, GB News, Adventures in Wonder on Prime Time, we were flooded with questions, hundreds of comments, and the same theme kept coming up again and again, which was money, everybody's favorite subject. How did you afford it? What did it cost? Are you secretly wealthy? So, I'm putting together a short series called The Cost of Freedom. And today, we're getting into the actual numbers. Let me address the assumption first. About 30% of the comments we received assumed we were wealthy. That there was a trust fund or an inheritance, some financial backstory we hadn't mentioned. There wasn't. What we had was a sailing school spent years building a house with a mortgage and the ability to add up. It's not sexy. It's just what happened when arithmetic met aspiration. Total working budget when we left in 2017 was about £175,000 which is about £222,000 US or £25,000 at today's rate. Not a vast sum for eight years and five people but enough if you're deliberate about how you spend it. And it has to be said at this point that other people have done it on far far less. The boat was a 1997 AML Super Maramu. We paid about £130,000 which is about 165,000 US or 152,000 euros which came primarily from selling the small holiday unit we had in the marina and liquidating the sailing school we had there too after we were hit by the financial crisis of 2008 and 2009 if you're old enough to remember that. So we sold the assets plowed the money into several smaller side hustles and eventually in 2017 converted all that into the boat. But the years leading up to that were spent quietly converting everything into that one pot. We did Etsy furniture builds, uh, eBay sales, Airbnb room rentals. None of them produced one big windfall. It was just a lot of smaller incomes that added up over time over just about a decade. We earmarked roughly £45,000 which is about 57,000 US or 53,000 for upgrades and first year running cost which turned out to be an underestimate because the first few years held more surprises than we'd actually budgeted for. That's actually the more honest version of how most people from this type of life. You're not waiting for the lottery. You're just systematically converting what you have into what you need. Converting assets into freedom. And that's probably the most accurate way to describe it. That's the cost of freedom. We kept a separate emergency fund of about £22,000, which is about 28,000 US or 26,000 untouchable in theory for medical emergencies, major mechanical failures, anything that could basically end the trip. In practice, watching it dip below what we considered acceptable levels was common and genuinely stressful. Rather than pay full insurance premiums, which became extortionate over time, despite only making one small claim, we opted for third party cover and self-insured the rest. Over 8 years, that saved us a considerable amount, but it meant the emergency fund was also our medical fund, our breakdown fund, and our safety net for everything. The thing that kept us genuinely stable throughout was the rental income from our house back in the UK. We didn't sell it. We rented it out which covered the mortgage and put roughly £1,300 or £1,700 US 1,500 a month into the budget. If you own a property, are considering doing something like this, I'd strongly recommend this route. The question everyone wants to know um what does it actually cost per month? And the honest answer is it varies enormously. That's not being me being vague, but it really does. It depends on where we were, what broke, whether we were in a marina or anchored in the middle of nowhere. In the final years of our trip, once we'd actually learned how to live this way efficiently, we settled into a roughly monthly budget of about £2,000, which is £2,500 US or €2,300. As I said, sometimes it was significantly more, like when we were in boatyards paying for repair work and upgrades. Sometimes it was significantly less like when we were at anchor in a remote at all. That was obviously more than our rental income from the property. The remainder largely being made up from patron income and YouTube ad revenue. To put that in context, medium household income works out roughly £2,900 a month in the UK or $6,900 a month in the US and approximately £3,500 across the EU based on a typical two adult household equivalent. But those numbers aren't directly comparable obviously because health care in the US often comes out of that monthly income while in the UK and the EU it's largely state funded and household and social support systems also vary significantly. But you get the idea. We were living on far less than the medium household income of any western country. Year one was obviously more expensive. You don't rock up on a boat and instantly know how to do this efficiently. There's a steep learning curve and steep learning curves inevitably cost money. But if you're currently spending on a mortgage, a commute, two cars, after school clubs, and all the rest of it, then simplifying significantly and living on the water isn't necessarily a more expensive option. The single biggest investment we made in reducing ongoing costs was the onboard systems, solar panels, lithium batteries, or water maker. Once these were in place, we spent far less on marina facilities. We weren't buying bottled water. We could anchor for free for weeks at a time. Those systems paid for themselves quickly. Although you do have to factor in the cost of maintaining them as well. Marinas are expensive. I think that goes without saying. Mediterranean marinas can run to 50 80 100 plus euros a night. Anchoring is free in most parts of the world and it's perfectly safe. We were in marinas when we had to be mainly for repairs, escaping bad weather or when there was no other option otherwise we were on the hook. We flew home about once in 8 years I think and even that was paid for by the documentary filmmakers that we were working for. That's a significant saving that people don't always factor in. Many cruising families blow large portions of their annual budget on flights back for Christmas or Easter or just general family gatherings, you know, birth of a child. We made the decision not to do that. And while it had an emotional cost, one of those costs of freedoms that I was talking about, it made a real financial difference. And maintenance, we did almost all of it ourselves. engine work, rigging, electrics, fiberglass repairs, oryard labor rates vary wildly reasonable in some countries and extortionate in others. And I'm looking at you Shelter Bay Marina in Panama on that one. Either way, if you don't have the skills when you leave, you acquire them out of necessity. DIY on a boat isn't optional. If you're trying to keep cost under control, it's pretty integral to the lifestyle. One more assumption worth addressing is that the boat is a luxury asset that we've been selling around on a floating investment that's gone up in value. Let me put that to bed straight away. This the secondhand blue water sailing market has been depressed for years. We've put significant amounts of money into a boat over the eight years. Upgrades, systems, repairs. The needle on its value has barely moved. It doesn't appreciate like a house. Think of it as a car, not a property. It's transport and shelter, not infrastructure, and certainly not an investment. The point is, we spend differently. The mental shift that made this all possible wasn't how do we generate more income. It was what are we currently spending money on and what of that needs to change. When you ask that question honestly, the answer can be surprising sometimes. Two cars, a bigger house, holidays that cost more than our entire monthly budget, stuff in general. We traded all of that for time with each other, time with the kids, time to actually go into the world rather than just talk about it or just visited it for a weekend or a week. There were hard times. I want to be honest about that. days where the money was genuinely worrying where we questioned whether we'd made a terrible mistake. That's very real and I don't think we ever glossed over that in our YouTube blogs, as you'll know if you'd followed them. But if this episode is making one point, it's probably more achievable than you think. Not easily. Nothing about what we did was easy. But if you're a homeowner with some equity, some assets you're willing to liquidate, and a genuine willingness to spend carefully, the arithmetic is not as impossible as you might think. So, just a reminder that everything I've covered here is in the book in considerably more detail and the captain's table members on Patreon will be getting early access to these chapters. Next week, the education question, what did we actually do about school for eight years? That fills the comment sections almost as much as money does. But for now, I hope this has helped. Thanks for listening and I'll see you next Thursday.

- Draw My Life - Coming To America: Kind: captions Language: en - A lot of you may not know this, but I actually was born in Russia. (speaks in a foreign language) - Don't nobody understand the words that are coming outta your mouth, man. - Bee-a-whoop, I'm a young five-year-old boy in Russia, and my parents tell me that in a couple weeks, we're about to move to the United States. I can't even comprehend what that means. When you're five, six, your world is, like, this big. My world was preschool, home, maybe the ice cream store. My parents tell me we're moving to America, gave me no information about why we're doing it, how long we're doing it for. They told me I'm not allowed to tell anybody, because it was a secret. I had no idea why this was. I thought they were playing a game with me, actually. We had only 24 hours of notice to our closest, closest friends to say that we were, in fact, leaving. Later on, I've come to realize that we came to the United States on a refugee status visa, I guess, it would say. That was our governmental status. We came here to have a better life for our family, for my sister, et cetera. I vividly remember the flight. Because it was my first time being on an airplane, I was extremely excited, also didn't speak English. My parents didn't speak English, but my sister did. So she was ordering drinks for everybody, even though all she was saying was Coke, Pepsi. I was like, okay, that's not really, you don't get credit for knowing how to say brand names, and I remember the flight attendant was so kind to me that she gave me like this little Delta pin that I was like, oh my God, in America, they give pins for free. I don't have to pay you for this. I didn't know if it was a trick. As a kid, you're like, new land, new people, don't know what's going on. I'm not gonna trust anybody, but once we landed, I remember my uncle coming to the airport. He gave me a gift of, like, hot wheels cars, and in Russia, we didn't have anything. I'm talking, like, I might have one color picture of me growing up. There was no fancy technology. There was no toys. A good birthday present was markers, and little did I know, markers dry out if you leave them in direct sunlight. I left it inside of a car, and I literally was crying because I had no markers, and that was my birthday present, and our cars, by the way, were terrible in Russia. There was a company called Zhiguli. It was like, ugh. If you think whatever car you have now is bad, take a sledge hammer to the rims, to the windows, okay, maybe not that bad, but abuse it for 20 years, and that's our brand new version of our car, and then we took the car ride over to Brooklyn where we were gonna live, and it was during Christmas time. It was early December that we came. So there was lights everywhere, and I was like, oh my God, there's lights. There's holiday spirit. It's so beautiful. Looking back at it now, it was a random neighborhood in Bay Ridge, Brooklyn that I'm looking at now, and I'm like, okay, it just looks fine. Our apartment was only a one bedroom apartment for my dad, my mom, my sister, and me. Our parents' bedroom was the living room, the bedroom was cut in half for me and my sister, and the kitchen served as like the little kitchen table that we ate at with four people, as well as a computer, because we needed a computer for my mom to be able to do some work and for my dad to go to medical school for the second time in his life, in his forties, in a new language. So this apartment was a disaster. The only thing that was great about it, actually, was that it had an enormously long hallway. Me and my dad ended up getting a plastic ball and playing soccer in that hallway kicking the ball back and forth to see who could score, and the more bounces you got across the walls, the easier it was to score, because it was hard to judge where it was gonna go. That was good times. By the way, this housing is like welfare housing, essentially. It was rent-controlled. I'm pretty sure it was $400 a month when we first moved in, maybe even $300 a month, cockroaches galore, mice galore. I was definitely afraid of these mice, because my mom was afraid. So I emulated her fear. You could hear the mice running around at night when we would watch television. So I came to the states, and I didn't go to school right away. We were planning on sending me to school, I think, right after the new year. So I came in the middle of first grade. Going into school when you don't speak the language, teacher's asking you questions right away, right? They want to get to know you. She asks me a question. I say one of the three words I know. I said, "Yes." She bought it. She comes in, asks me another question. I say the second word that I know, "No." She buys it. She asks me another follow-up question. I say, "Maybe." Now she realizes this dude does not speak English. I need to find a translator. Luckily there was two girls in my class, twins, Rita and Rosa, who spoke Russian and English, and they translated. They understood where I could hang my jacket. I was obviously very stressed out coming to a new school, meeting new people. I cried the first day and the second day and the third day. When our parents came to pick us up, my mom came. The teacher grabbed my mom and was telling her, your son is crying a lot. I wanna make sure he's okay, this and that. My mom has no idea what she's saying, because my mom doesn't speak any English yet. Cry, sad, they're using words, sign language, anything they can essentially show to each other that I'm upset, and they want me to feel better. Blah, blah, blah, we figure it out. The one thing I didn't struggle with, even in first grade when I first came over, was math. Because my mom was a math teacher, and in Russia, they're like, you better know everything there is to know about math as a six-year-old, I had the knowledge of a third grader of math. So math was super easy, and I could, sort of, coast on the math component and try really hard on the English and everything else that I was terrible at, and I remember going to ESL and then my grandparents picking me up from school and then telling my grandma like, "I learned a new word today." She's like, "What'd you learn?" I was like, "Hot air balloon." She's like, "What is that?" I'm like, "Oh, it's a thing that flies in the air." My grandpa actually, and my grandma, religiously picked me up from school from grades one to three. After that, they said you're old enough to cut it in New York City. Good luck as a nine-year-old, and we started walking home as a nine-year-old. Luckily I lived one to two blocks away from school. So it was a very short route for me. I had some close friends growing up, some were Russian, some were not. They all lived in the same vicinity, usually in the same building. We played handball a lot. That was the number one sport go to, and then as I started becoming more interested in American culture, I got really into basketball and watching like Michael Jordan and the Bulls play. So I would watch it with my dad, and then my dad took me and got me this, it was like a children's ball, but it had the Bulls logo on it. I treasured it. It was trash, though, like a piece of rubber. It'd bounce too much, and when you threw it against the rim, it bounce like 50 yards back, but then when I went to go play with the regular kids who've been playing basketball their whole lives and they played with the real basketball, I was terrible, because I was getting used to playing with a five inch ball and now I'm playing with a legit-size 29 and a half inch ball. So that was, sort of, my childhood initially coming over as an immigrant. A lot of kids teased you. That was, kind of, the norm when you came from another country. I don't know why it never made me that upset. I think it was because our class was so diverse and so mixed that it seemed like everyone was getting made fun of for something. That just happened to be my thing. Something that also helped, I started doing Taekwondo when I was younger. My parents saved up enough money to literally pay the tuition of the Taekwondo school. That was the max that they could do. They were like, "Okay, you have one after-school activity. "It's Taekwondo. "We want you to do that. "We want you to be in good shape." (people screaming comically) An activity that I had with my dad to do a little father-son bonding when he had time at night was to go onto the street called 86th street in Bay Ridge, Brooklyn that had a lot of stores on it and plan out some of the stuff we hoped to buy when we had some money. One of the stores that we used to go to was called Nobody Beats the Wiz. (upbeat rap music) ♪ Nobody beats the wiz ♪ - and it was an electronics store that sold boom boxes, televisions, they had open box sales, whatever. It was basically like the Best Buy of today, but it wasn't as nice. We were never able to afford anything there, except once, we did buy an open-box boom box, meaning someone bought it, returned it, and it was still open, so we took it, and it was like 200 bucks, and it was the biggest purchase we ever had. Other stores on that journey that we used to go to that I loved, 99 Cent Store, our best friend. I'm talking we bought everything at the 99 Cents Store. Oh, pizza was less than a dollar a slice and Sal's Pizzeria in Brooklyn was the top for me. Brooklyn pizza, nothing tops it. Sorry, Chicago, wherever else people think that there's good pizza. The cheap 75 cents slice that looks like it has oil artificially splurged all over it, delicious. Oh, the number one shoe store that I had all my shoes from, Payless, boom. Love Payless. If it wasn't for Payless, I would never have new shoes. As you could tell, it was a lot of stores where it was humbling, but it's only humbling looking back now, because back then, I thought Payless Shoes was a dope brand. There's so many more options than what we had in Russia. So what you have here and you may think is bad could be way better than what we have in other countries, and I think that's sometimes lost in the messaging of my social media content. People think like, he's successful. That means he was always successful, or he probably comes from a wealthy family. That's furthest from the truth. I had a great family, very supportive family who worked very hard, educated in two countries. There was a lot of work put into that. There was a lot of times where we were on the bottom, bottom, bottom, and in order to get to where we are today, we had to put in a lot of work, and I want you to not lose sight of that, because I think the beauty of where we live here in the United States is that opportunity is available for you. You have to be smart. You have to make some wise choices, and you may mess up, and you may have setbacks, but if you keep working at it, I really think America's the best place for increased opportunities. I mentioned my sister earlier. Well, here's a full video with me and my sister to see how well she knows me. Watch it. It's a blast. She basically roasts me the whole time. As always, stay happy and healthy. I'll see you on this video, though.

- The “Yachting” Trap: Why Most People Who Buy Yachts Are Broke Within 3 Years: Kind: captions Language: en A used 58-ft sportfish boat sells in North Carolina. The sale price is $1 million. The seller walks away having paid $100,000 in broker commissions he never knew were fixed. His buyer's broker, the one he thought was working for him, collected $50,000 of that paid by the seller and never disclosed it. That was the cheap part of the deal. The closing check was smaller than the first year's operating bill. This video is about the financial structure behind that moment and why it repeats itself on marina docks from Florida to the Mediterranean every single season. The yachting trap is not one thing. It is a layered system with five moving parts and every part is designed to profit from someone who does not know the math. Who actually gets rich in the yachting world and is it ever the owner? Polished hulls gleam beneath showroom lights. Brokers in pressed blazers glide between espresso bars and leather sofas speaking in the practice language of possibility. The pitch is never about the boat itself. It is about a new identity. Here, the yacht is a passport to freedom, to status, to a kind of belonging that only exists dockside. The buyer, often successful in every other arena, steps into this world with a mix of calculation and longing. The brochures promise more than a vessel. They promise transformation. Words like investment, equity, and legacy drift through the conversation, but the real hook is always lifestyle. Brokers talk about Mediterranean summers, long weekends in the Bahamas, the silent glide out of the marina at sunrise. They mention family memories, business deals sealed on the off deck, friendships cemented over sunset cocktails. There is an unspoken suggestion that this is a club with velvet ropes and the only ticket is the signature at the bottom of the contract. For a moment, the buyer can almost see it. The gentle sway at anchor, the applause of water against the hull, the quiet certainty of having arrived. The pen hovers over the dotted line. The numbers, what they mean and what they hide, are for later. For now, the dream is real and the only thing left is to step aboard. The numbers the showroom never mentions are the ones that matter most. First, the baseline. Annual operating costs run between 10 and 15% of the yacht's original purchase price. That means for a $500,000 boat, the owner should expect to spend 50 to 75,000 dollars every single year just to keep the vessel afloat, insured, fueled, and maintained. [music] No exotic breakdowns, no storms, just the routine bill for showing up at the dock. Next, usage. The US Coast Guard's most recent survey puts average recreational boat [music] use at about 55 days per year. That's less than eight weeks out of 52. Most owners get on the water for a handful of weekends and a single extended trip, if that. The rest of the year, the yacht sits idle accumulating costs while losing value. Then comes depreciation. Industry-wide data show a new yacht loses between 10 and 20% of its value in the first year alone. By the fifth year, the typical vessel has shed 40 to 50% of its original price. This is not an outlier, it's the expected curve regardless of brand or maintenance. For a $500,000 boat, that is $250,000 gone before the first serious refit. These three benchmarks, 10 to 15% in annual costs, 55 days of use, and up to half the value lost in 5 years, form the backbone of the real ownership equation. They are not worst-case scenarios, but the industry's own consensus drawn from broker guides and Coast Guard data. The dream is sold in broad strokes, but the ledger is written in fine print. Charter programs are often pitched as the rational antidote to yachting's notorious cash burn. The promise is simple. Put your boat to work, let paying guests cover the bills, and enjoy the best of both worlds. The reality is governed by thresholds that most owners never cross. According to Vital Charters internal modeling, the break-even point for a typical 60-ft motor yacht lands between 16 and 20 weeks of paid charter per year. Anything less and the owner is subsidizing every charter guest out of pocket. The national average for private use, about 55 days a year, falls well short of this threshold and few owners have the appetite or network to fill a third of the calendar with paying clients. Stretch the lens to a decade. A $3 million yacht run through a standard charter management program generates a total outflow of roughly $8.1 million over 10 years. That figure absorbs purchase price, depreciation, operating costs, management fees, and the periodic refits that come with heavy use. The number is not a worst-case scenario. It is the sum of routine invoices, insurance renewals, and the relentless march of marina rate hikes. Charter income, even at a healthy utilization rate, rarely claws back more than a fraction of this drain. The illusion comes from gross revenue. Charter brochures spotlight the top line. $30,000 a week booked solid for the season. But after management commissions, unscheduled repairs, and the high wear cost curve of commercial use, the net is a shadow of the headline figure. Meanwhile, the same $3 million placed in a broad market index fund would have doubled over the same period compounding quietly while the yacht's value eroded. The math is not subtle. Over a decade, the real contest is not between yachting and doing nothing. It is between the dream of ownership and the opportunity cost of capital. The numbers do not just favor the market, they expose the lifestyle pitch as a system designed to extract, not to build, wealth for the typical buyer. A $500,000 yacht purchase, mapped line by line, reveals the real mechanics of ownership. The initial outlay is rarely just the down payment. With a standard 25% down, the buyer wires $125,000 to the dealer on day one. The remaining $375,000 is financed, usually at 5% to 6% interest over 5 to 10 years. Year one begins with a silent deduction, depreciation. Industry data puts the first year loss at 15% of the original price. $75,000 erased before the first season ends. The boat is now worth $425,000 on paper, but the loan principal remains untouched. Operating expenses arrive monthly. The 10% rule, accepted by every major brokerage, means $50,000 vanishes over 12 months for dockage, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and the steady drip of marina fees. These are not luxury upgrades or emergencies, just the baseline for keeping the vessel ready. Add annual interest on the loan, which at 5% means roughly $19,000 in year one alone. The running total, $75,000 lost to depreciation, $50,000 spent on operations, $19,000 paid in interest, and $125,000 gone in the initial down payment. The first 12 months consumed $269,000 of cash and equity. Year two repeats the cycle. Another $50,000 for operations, another $18,000 in the loan balance declines, and an additional 12% depreciation, about $51,000, shrinks the boat's value further. By the end of year three, the owner faces a cumulative outflow approaching $400,000, not including the original purchase The boat itself, now three years older, has lost up to 40% of its value trading at $300,000 to $320,000 on the open market. A looming cost sits just over the horizon. Major refits, engines, hull, electronics arrive around the five-year mark often demanding another 10% to 20% of the original price. For many, the decision to sell comes just before this grenade goes off, locking in losses and passing the next round of expenses to the next owner. The ledger does not lie. The system extracts value from the buyer at every step and the numbers compound quietly year after year. Brokers occupy a unique position in the yachting ecosystem. Their income is tied directly to the deal. A standard 10% commission is split evenly between the listing broker and the buyer's broker. On a $1 million sale, that is $100,000, $50,000 to each side. The seller pays the commission, but the buyer's broker is presented as their advocate. In reality, both brokers are incentivized to close the transaction, not to challenge its wisdom or interrogate the numbers. The commission is not a bonus for exceptional service. It is a built-in cost baked into every contract regardless of whether the buyer sails away with a sound investment or a rapidly depreciating asset. This arrangement is no secret within the industry, but it is rarely spelled out for buyers. The International Yacht Brokers Association, or IYBA, which sets the tone for much of the US market, has historically defended the 10% split as fair compensation for expertise and deal management. Yet, the lack of transparency has led to legal scrutiny. In 2023, the Lamb versus IYBA antitrust case landed in federal court. The claim was that IYBA members conspired to fix commissions at a flat $50,000 on $1 million transactions. Internal communications cited in the complaint described the fee as fixed [music] and non-negotiable, a direct challenge to the idea of open competition. The case remains unresolved, but its existence confirms that these commission structures are not just tradition. They are protected by industry consensus and at times enforced through opaque agreements. For buyers, this means the person guiding them through the process is paid by the other side and rewarded for closing quickly, not for ensuring long-term value. The advice chain is only as honest as the incentives allow. The moment the contract is signed and the commission is wired, any alignment between buyer and broker dissolves. The system is not accidental. It is engineered to move product, not to build wealth for the person stepping onto the dock for the first time. Referrals and incentives shape every step of yacht ownership. When a buyer asks for a marine survey, the broker's recommendation often comes with quiet expectations. Surveyors who keep deals moving forward are favored, while those who flag too many issues risk being left out of future business. The inspection, paid for by the buyer, becomes less about uncovering problems and more about protecting the sale. Loyalty flows through the broker's network, not to the owner's interests. Charter management follows the same logic. The promise is that chartering will offset costs, but the contract tells a different story. Management fees, locked in at 15 to 20% of gross charter revenue, are deducted before the owner sees a dollar. For every $100,000 earned, $15,000 to $20,000 is claimed by the management company, regardless of whether the owner turns a profit. The contract spells this out, but it is rarely the focus in sales pitches. >> [music] >> The manager's priority is bookings, not the owner's bottom line. Marina contracts add another layer. Annual escalation clauses, often buried in the fine print, quietly raise berth fees by 3 to 5% each year. A $1,500 monthly slip can reach nearly $1,700 by the third year. These increases compound, are rarely discussed up front, and only become clear with the next invoice. Escalation is rarely explained at the point of sale. Tax deductions are dangled as a final incentive. The so-called second home deduction is tightly limited. The yacht must qualify as a residence, >> be used for more than 14 days, and at least 10% of rental days, and survive IRS scrutiny. Most owners do not qualify, but the myth persists at the point of sale. Deduction. Language is used to close deals, not to guarantee savings. Each mechanism, survey referral, charter fee, marina escalation, tax myth, ensures service providers get paid first. The owner absorbs the risk and rising costs. Owner A stepped into the marina with a plan that looked solid on paper. The purchase price was $600,000. The down payment was $150,000. The rest was $450,000 financed at 6% interest over 10 years. The first season brought the expected joys, family weekends, a handful of business outings, the sense of having arrived. But by the end of year one, the numbers had already started to slip out of reach. The boat's value dropped by $90,000, 15%, before the first annual service was even due. Operating costs came in right on cue at $60,000, matching the 10% rule quoted by every brokerage. Loan interest alone added $27,000 to the tally. In 12 months, $177,000 was gone, and the boat's market value had slipped to $510,000. Year two, the pattern repeated. Another $60,000 in operating costs, another $25,000 in interest, and resale value fell by another 12%, $61,000 evaporated from the resale value. By the end of year three, the boat was worth $360,000. The loan balance stood at $415,000. Selling meant cutting a check just to close out the debt. The owner's equity had been hollowed out by a system that front-loads loss and back-loads risk. This is not a cautionary tale about poor planning. It is the statistical median for financed buyers who enter at retail and exit before the first major refit. Owner B believed in the charter offset. The pitch was simple. Buy an $850,000 catamaran, sign with a management company, and let paying guests cover the bills. Year one delivered $62,000 in gross charter revenue. Management fees, set at 20%, took $12,400 off the top. Heavy charter use drove up maintenance, $28,000 spent on repairs, and accelerated wear. That left $21,600 in net income against $127,000 in total ownership costs for the year. The ledger did not lie. The charter program covered less than a fifth of the actual outflow. By year two, bookings dropped, the charter firm terminated the contract, and the owner was left with a high-hour boat, mounting expenses, and no path to break even. The promise of neutralizing costs through chartering [music] had failed, not because of a bad season, but because the math was never in the owner's favor. Both owners started with different strategies, one with a classic loan, the other with a charter plan. Both ended up in the same place, chasing sunk costs, boxed in by a system that rewards everyone except the person holding the keys. The numbers are not outliers, they are the rule, disguised by glossy marketing and the language of opportunity. Owner C walked into the marina with no illusions about financing or fast returns. This time, there was no bank involved, just a wire transfer for the full price of a lightly used sloop, $220,000 paid in cash. On paper, it looked like a safe harbor. No loan, no interest, no risk of being underwater. But the map was still wrong. The first year brought an unexpected $32,000 in deferred maintenance. The survey had missed corroded rigging and a leaking fuel tank, issues quietly overlooked in the rush to close. The owner paid out of pocket, chalking it up to bad luck, but the pattern was already set. Insurance, which had seemed like a simple checkbox, turned into a moving target. By year two, the underwriter flagged the hull's age and refused renewal without costly upgrades. The only replacement policy available came with a $15,000 annual premium, more than double the original quote. The marina, meanwhile, had its own escalation clause. The slip fee, $700 a month at signing, rose to $1,000 by the third year, a 43% jump triggered by a clause buried on page six of the contract. The owner, now locked into the berth, watched fixed costs outpace any savings from buying with cash. A three-way cross-section tells the story. Owner A, boxed in by loan payments and a shrinking asset. Owner B, squeezed by charter math and management fees. Owner C, supposedly immune, still drained by hidden maintenance, insurance hikes, and rising marina costs. The specifics differ, but the destination is the same. Whether the entry point is debt, charter, or cash, the system's incentives ensure that costs compound quietly and persistently, while value erodes. The only constant across all profiles is that the service providers, brokers, surveyors, managers, marina operators, get paid first. The owner, regardless of strategy, absorbs the risk and the loss. The pattern is not accidental. It is engineered by contracts, clauses, and a network of incentives that operate in plain sight. The promise of yachting as a rational asset dissolves when the numbers are laid side by side. What remains is a lifestyle product, sold as an investment, but structured so that the owner is always the last to get paid. Today, the illusion persists. Glossy brochures still frame yachts as assets, not consumables. The real math, depreciation rates exceeding 20% per year, operating costs rivaling mortgage payments, remains buried in fine print. In a market engineered for extraction, clarity is power. The only real windfall belongs to those who read the ledger, not just the dream. The map was never neutral. Now you know where it leads.

- Why Aman Gupta and His boAt Failed?: Kind: captions Language: en The boat is really big. The boat has now become Titanic. We just found the Titanic. What is your MOT? Aman Gupta has asked this question to thousands of founders. A founder finishes his pitch with full authority in front of cameras on live TV. Well, this question comes like a bullet. Simple Direct Devastating. Mot means that one thing which creates such a moat around your company that the competition cannot copy it even if it wants to. It could be technology, it could be distribution. Any genuine barrier is the real defense mechanism that actually keeps a business afloat when everything else is falling. So let us directly ask about the same company which made Aman the startup poster boy of India. India's number one audio brand, billion dollar unicorn, over $ crore customers, National Creator Award from PM Modi. Now what is the motive of this company? Total 101 engineers in a company worth Rs 3000 crore. Only God knows how much of this is in RAD. Because RAD spend is only 1.5% of revenue. Think for yourself what you will develop. There are no technology barriers, no product modes, nothing that makes genuine copying impossible. Revenue growth has been stagnant for the last 3 years. Have been trying to launch an IPO for 4 years and have been failing. And new competitors like Bolt are coming from behind and growing sales at a speed of 40%. This company is Aman Gupta's boat which is sinking and Aman Gupta has already left the sinking boat and fled in his new boat Offbreet. This means you spend Rs 450 before selling your product to a new customer. Basically, there is a customer acquisition cost of 450 boats and this is a pure loss. Because you cannot recover Rs 450 by selling it once. You will have to sell it at least four to five times. But 72 out of 100 customers do not return. So this becomes their net loss. No customer loyalty and then a press release came in June 2023. This year our net sales are approximately Rs 4000 crore. Published in Economic Times. Lindin went viral. People also believed it. Then the audit came out to be ₹3285 crores meaning a difference of ₹715 crores. The press release came first and the audit came later, no one checked it because the story was so good that no one wanted to verify it. Wrongdoings and manipulations were done to show a good growth story and brother, their own auditors BSR & Company are saying that the company's quarterly bank statements and the company's own books do not match. Completely red fact, that too not for one, not for two, not for three years continuously every year. What's going on brother, seriously? And all this is just because for 5 years from 2018 to 2023, growth was going like a rocket in the same direction. 300% 150% 119% Everything was going on in the storm. When the storm stopped, everything started coming to light one by one. Revenue growth in FY 2023 was only 14%, in FY 2024 -5%, in FY 2025 - 1.6%, 3 years revenue almost remained the same, around 3000 crores, the boat stopped and yes it was a profit making company till 2023, for the first time in 2023, net loss was 130 crores, after that hardly ever made a profit. Smart watches in the first place. Look, Boat's smart watch revenue in 2023 was Rs 900 crore. 27% of total revenue Everyone felt that they had found a long term revenue source. But by 2025 it will be Rs 330 crore. 63% disappeared in just 2 years because 128 no-name brands entered the market and made watches by purchasing parts from the same Chinese manufacturers. The average selling price fell from Rs 5,000 to Rs 2,000. Meanwhile, Tech Burner had also launched its NARC and the situation was very bad. The only session Boat had was product innovation because anyone can sell Vibe. No one can copy technology. Noise grabbed double the market share in smartwatches and Realme in earbuds, for which audio is a side product. Comes bundled with the phone. Three was leaving behind a side product boat whose core business wasn't audio because anyone could sell Vibe. Technology cannot be copied. Elsewhere, Data March 2024, Delhi, PM Modi presented the National Creator Award to Aman Gupta. Stage Pe Photo Video India's Official Best Creator Publicly This pick was of everything. Right? Exactly 31 days later, in April 2024, a hacker hacked the bot's entire customer database. 7.5 million people. If you have ever purchased a Boat product, then your data like name, number, address, email etc. is available on the dark web. Meanwhile, Person 24 Boat on 180 Bus acquired your data by spending Rs 450. Someone sold it for 0.0024. Because brother, it takes money to build cyber security infrastructure, right? And we invest money only in AIDS. Third place is employee attrition at 34%, with one in three employees leaving every year. And in fact, three CEOs came every three years. Look, it was being eaten by termites from four places and amidst all this, the company played its last card, IPO, that too twice and both the times something happened which you would never have expected. January 2022. Boat files IPO with SEBI to raise ₹2000 crore. The company was profitable. Growth was 100% plus. SEBI also approved it. Everything was set but the IPO plan was dead. Because Boat found an investor in its IPO who was investing at the high price demanded by it, so that Boat's dream of becoming a unicorn could be fulfilled. Unicorn means a startup whose valuation is $1 billion or more. But Warbuck Pincus' investment came but the market situation was not suitable for bringing an IPO. Second attempt after 3 years in 2025 but no big investor is ready to invest money. Independent analysts have even said that the valuation of the boat has come down to Rs 350 to 400 million. Meaning they are asking for a 70% discount from their 1.3 billion valuation. The warbird who invested at a valuation of 1 billion wants to exit but is in deep trouble. He also invested in Vibe and not in Fail. When no one wants to buy, this is the biggest signal. The IPO did not come again. Once Deferred, Twice Deferred Today is April 2026. No confirmed date, no price band. Then in September 2025, Aman Gupta, fed up, stepped down from the company. Along with him, his CEO also stepped down and then started Offbit. New company then one thing happened which defines everything. Besmer Venture Partners 100 crore who raised Lindin Fund, Shop Fund, Cva Fund. One of the most credible venture funds in the world, they invested money not in boats but in Aman. When the world's top VCs invest in founders and not in the company, you know where the real value always lay. The question that Aman asked thousands of founders confidently in front of cameras on national TV: What is your motive? The answer to this is in SEBI documents. 3 years of audit flags, two failed IPO attempts, founders exited. And the world's most respected Visi invested in the name of the founders, not in the company. And now Boat is being handled by its third CEO Gaurav Nair. His consultancy experience is completely fresh. So the answer to the question has come. Was Boat ever truly a product company? Or did you mistake marketing for innovation? The documents told me, the numbers told me , Bejumar told me. A brand without a moat is a billboard, not a business. And people pass by the billboard. People stay in business. Boat created India's largest billboard. Stars, Singers, Sportsmen, Comedians, 2 Crore Boat Huts Community reached every home. The name boat was given so that it would float but without an engine the boat drifts. Brother Aman Gupta is a genius, he raised Rs 100 crore without disclosing the product and created a company worth Rs 400 crore on the very first day. So does Aman Gupta want to become India's Steve Jobs? Will Offbit become the next Apple? Please let me know in the comments and also tell me which product you are using after replacing the boat ? But the reality is that this company has become a huge marketing gimmick. Of all the revenue that Apple earns, 6% is spent on marketing. Of all the revenue Samsung earns, only 10% is spent on marketing. But of all the revenue generated by the boat, only 3.6% was spent on marketing. That too within 2022. By 2023, this number increased to 12.7%. And as of today, this number has gone up to 30%. Meaning, if this boat product is worth ₹100, then an ad worth ₹30 will have to be run on it. And why are these things happening? Because these people are not able to innovate. No product is a company. This is a marketing company that I have spoken about a long time ago. Their spend on RAD is 1% of their revenue. Now some people will feel that 1% of Rs 3000 crore is too much. Brother, Apple's monthly sales are worth billions of dollars. Still their spend on RAD is 6 to 7%. Samsung's spend on RAD is 8 to 9%. Sony also has 6 to 7%. Why? Because they are hardware companies. Those are actually production companies and this is a marketing company. As long as you are able to run ads , you are able to sell. The day the ad stops running or the ad budget is reduced , sales also drop. As of today, they have shifted some of their manufacturing to India. Started making some things in India. But there is no innovation in that either. The same Chinese things are being repeated. She took out her smart watch and is facing downfall. She took out the ring and is facing downfall. They do not have any new USP or product. Whose future is coming? Coming to the glasses. And they haven't even started exploring inside the glasses yet. If the company is doing it internally then I don't know but till now they have not announced anything openly and around 2023 they had announced that our company is making a revenue of Rs 4000 crores. Then in November 2023, MCA audit takes place and the numbers are actually found out. It seems that there was no revenue of Rs 4000 crore. The revenue was ₹3370 crore and its net loss was ₹129 crore, which was the first time in eight years. There was a gap of ₹623 crore between the numbers actually claimed by the boat company and the actual numbers. Because of which what did people start doing? People started considering their numbers as a PR stunt. People stopped believing what he said. And when they tried to file for IPO, their accounts were audited. It was then seen that they had calculated short term borrowings within long term subsidiary needs. Its Singapore subsidiary was operating in loss. And during the audit it was found that they had also evaluated Section 197 within 2023. What happens is that a salary cap is imposed on the employees of the company. Because let's assume that my company is making a profit of ₹1000 crores. If I say my salary, I took all the profit of ₹1000 crore. Or should I say my salary is ₹110 crores. The company is running at a loss of Rs 10 crore. So the numbers ca n't be manipulated too crazily. Therefore some caps are installed. And the cab is charged on the basis of the size of the company. A total of nine red flakes were found in their books and their balance sheet was not matching. Balance sheet matching is taught in 11th class. proximity to Clear Lake which is considered by many to be the Boating capital of Texas besides being a beautiful water destination of itself Clear Lake is a gateway to Galveston Bay and ultimately the Gulf of Mexico many people in the area own boats and as a result I've had many Boat Owners coming into my office who are involved in divorce asking me what will happen to the boat assuming your boat was purchased during the marriage and also assuming you're trying to come up with a settlement plan for the division of your community estate then you have three basic options option one sell the boat the most common way to address a boat in the lead up to divorce is to sell the boat and then split the proceeds with your soon to be ex-spouse but this option presents some challenges not only must you find a willing buyer but you also likely must accept the depreciated value for the vessel as such your boat may be worth more to you than its sale price suggests option two keep the boat if you love your boat you may not want anyone anyone else to own it but you really have to be honest with yourself and determine if continued boat ownership is financially feasible especially since after the divorce you'll only have your own income to rely on things you have to consider are where will you keep the boat if you currently own your own dock will you have that particular piece of real estate after the divorce and if not marinas are an option but they're expensive though they are convenient a mooring is less uh less is another option and it's less expensive but also kind of inconvenient and a trailer is generally the cheapest option but the most inconvenient another thing to consider is maintenance and repair they've got to be considered and they're not cheap especially in salt water the boat as much now that you're no longer married after divorce what family of friends will regularly join you on your trips after divorce will you be experiencing any changes in the demands of your own time that'll leave less time for boating such as now having to transport your children or you having to work overtime or you're now just living further away from your boat if after careful consideration and a little spreadsheet calculation you decide that you would have the ability to keep your boat after divorce then you have to decide how you plan to buy out your spouse's interest in the boat you likely we'll have to come up with enough cash to buy out your spouse's ownership interest basically you'll have to buy your own boat back with the remaining portion of the community estate if you own other items of significant value that you're willing to part with and your spouse wants you may be able to exchange your interest in those to compensate for the value of the boat either way your boat will have to be valued unless your spouse agrees the amount you'll have to determine the objective value your spouse will want to Value the boat at its greatest possible amount while you're going to want to evaluate at its lowest depreciated value ing boats in a volatile market and in an uncertain economy can be difficult it may require the services of a professional appraiser option three share the boat even though your marriage is coming to an end you may be on good terms with your spouse if both you and your partner want to continue using the boat you can the vessel and you're going to need to carefully draft the terms of your co-ownership in your divorce decree if your divorce involves difficult assets like boats then you should be sure you consult And so, I'm going to go to the yacht rigger to select from their massive inventory of running rigging and pick the best line for the job. we'll be able to have Stephen help us out with that and make sure that we get the right thing concerning this boat and maybe you'll learn a few things about running rigging for your own boat. And then the second thing that I need to do is I need to get the sails on this boat. She has been basically uh a neaked mast this entire time. So it hasn't had sales on it in 12 years. I do have the sails. They're up in storage. So I need to bring those down. And the third thing I need to do and pretty important thing is that I've got to deal with the fact that I have added what feels like thousands but whatever maybe hundreds of objects I've put inside this boat to make it livable. I've got to do what I call operation stick it. I've got to go pick a corner of this boat and start problem solving to get everything set up so that when I heal over for the first time I don't send I don't know my PlayStation 5 flying across the galley and hit someone in the face. don't feel like that would be great. So, those three things are what's going to happen. I'm going to be uploading a video, like I said, every single day. And uh I hope you follow along in this. I hope that there's information in here that's helpful for you guys. But if nothing else, we'll see whether or not I sink or swim in this endeavor at my place in my life, 40 years old and starting over. And with that said, I will see you guys tomorrow.

Uh and several individuals including Alexey Mikhailov and Natalia Mishina, uh Alexander Vietkin, and uh and like I said, there's another there's a whole list of people which I'll put on screen. So, the suit targets both US and international corporate affiliates and individual principals tied to Bering branded yacht manufacturing or sales. So, the contract at the center of the dispute, according to the complaint, Angeli's and Bering signed a new yacht construction agreements on the December the 15th, 2021. Like the yacht like I said, a 22.8-m or 75-ft custom steel and aluminum explorer yacht uh built to stringent charter requirements and equipped with advanced systems including silent mode technology, solar integration, custom HVAC, and Veem propellers just to name a few. And the delivery date in the contract was December 30th, 2023 unless extended properly under contract rules. What does that mean? If there's a problem if there's a problem sourcing some equipment, uh if there's on that's on the side of the manufacturer, if the people don't, you know, pay the milestone payments in time, uh then there can be delays, but they're usually done under contract and both sides agree on them. So, what went wrong? The complaint states that Angeli's paid approximately 83% of the purchase price and Bering allegedly completed only about 70% of the vessel. Construction was not completed by the contractual deadline and Bering allegedly requested advances on milestone payments in March 2025 citing current market conditions and lack of new sales. Angeli's declined without a detailed updated construction schedule. According to the complaint, Bering never produced that detailed schedule. As a result, Angeli's claims loss of charter revenue, cancellation of pre-booked charters, reputational damage, and systems on board becoming obsolete or out of warranty due to delays. Now, the most serious allegations, the complaint alleges that all Bering branded companies across North Carolina, Florida, Turkey, and Bulgaria operated as one unified enterprise, not separate companies. Against the individual defendants, the complaint alleges that undercapitalizing companies, siphoning corporate money for personal use, using company credit cards for personal interior design, using business assets for private residences, and swapping shares to conceal or facilitate these actions. Now, because of this alleged siphoning of funds, Angeli's claims that the shipyard entities were left unable to complete the yacht, manage the deadlines, and uphold financial obligations under the construction agreement. So, what is Angeli's asking for the court for? Angeli's asked the court to order Bering to complete the vessel as specified, breach of contract damages for losses including charter revenue, loss of use, obsolescence of onboard equipment, and other financial harm. They want the court to hold all Bering entities and the individual defendants personally liable for the obligations under the contract. So, what does that mean? This is one of the rare times a yacht owner has asked the federal court to merge over a dozen international companies into one legal actor and hold executives personally responsible for alleged misuse of funds. If the plaintiffs' claims are proven, the case could have industry-wide implications for how yacht builders could structure their international operations, transparency in milestone payments, and accountability for delays in high-value yacht projects. Just one thing about milestone payments, what that basically means is you put down a deposit, they start to build the yacht. When they've built a certain part of the yacht or they've certain they've got to a certain point in the construction phase, then they pay the next milestone payment, the next, you know, the next installments, then they continue to build it and so on until the yacht is finished and then before just before delivery, they pay the final amount. All right, guys. That's all we've got for now. We've got another update coming probably next week, so stay tuned for that. Just one more thing before I go, the nominations for the 2025 Superyacht of the Year or SOTY as we call it are on our YouTube channel ready for you to vote. Uh there's two categories that you can vote in. One of them is the best new superyacht for 2025. And the four vessels on that list are motoryacht Breakthrough from Feadship, motoryacht Shackleton from Lürssen, slight mistake there putting that on cuz it's not actually been delivered yet, but it's too late to change now. It's a it's an impressive-looking yacht though. And Leviathan by Ocean Co. This is Gabe Newell's new yacht. And motoryacht Dreamboat, the new Dreamboat of course. Um so yeah, so that's the four for the newest new best new superyacht of 2025. And the this is the final four for the overall superyacht of 2025 as seen on our channel on on the Yacht Report channel. - The Dark Side of Superyachts: SCAMS You Won’t Believe!: Kind: captions Language: en But the phones were dead. They'd been scammed. They paid the scammers $6,000. [Music] Super yacht recruitment generally happens quite quickly. What tends to happen is somebody on board the yacht quits while they're on leave, which leaves the yacht scrambling to find a replacement. I've been on a phone interview with the captain and he's offered me the job right there and then. And when I've said, "When do you need me?" he said, "Today, yesterday. When can you get here?" Because of the logistics involved, the yacht being in one country, the management company in another, and the recruitment agency somewhere else, all of the recruitment is generally done remotely, on the phone, and via email. Usually, the first contact will come from an agency, recruitment agency. They'll check that you've got the right certificates, your passport's up to date, your medicals are up to date, your STCW, your life survival, things like that, they're all up to date. Once they're happy with that, you'll be passed onto the yacht itself. So, the next contact usually comes from the vessel that you're going to work on. Could be the captain, could be a head of department, or it could be the person that you're replacing on the vessel. They'll talk to you about the job itself. They'll make sure you've got the proper experience and they'll just try to get a handle on you as a person to see if they think you would fit on board in that tight community. If they think you're a good fit, you'll most likely hear again from the recruitment agency or the management company and they will tell you you've been successful. Now, if the vessel has one, most large yachts do, you'll hear from the purser who will send you things contracts to sign, flight details, and any other data you might require. This can all happen really quickly, sometimes just in a few days. David and Linda, not their real names, they were looking for a job where they could go as a couple. David had four years of yachting experience, some of that as a deck officer on a smaller yacht. Linda also had a few years of experience. So, both of them experienced in yachting, but still both of them only 24 years old. They were both from the US. Couples hiring on yachts is quite rare. Yachts prefer to hire single people because they don't want to deal with the extra issues such as with accommodation. Now, sometimes they will recruit a captain and a chief stew couple because the captain would normally have a single occupancy cabin anyway. That's one less cabin they need to assign to that person. But, David was not a captain and Linda was not a chief stewardess. They saw this job on a Facebook page, private Facebook page called Fort Lauderdale Yachties. They were contacted initially by a company called Poseidon Yacht Services in ACI Marina in Croatia. They were told the vessel that they'd applied for was a 48-m yacht based in Croatia. And David and Linda were sent application forms and Schengen visa forms, which is required for crew from outside of Europe who wish to work on boats based in Europe. After they found filled in all the forms and returned them, a labor broker contacted them and conducted an interview over the phone. Then the chief stewardess from the yacht called Linda and spoke to her and asked her lots of stewardess related questions, how to carry out certain procedures, all common in yachting interviews. David was contacted also by a member of the crew and quizzed on how to clean the decks and various other work on board. You know, again, testing his skills that he he had the right skills to take on this job. This went on for 2 days. David asked if they'd recruited anybody else as well, just out of curiosity, and they said, "Yes, of course." They sent through an application form from a previous person that was partially redacted to show him that they'd placed others. So, David and Linda were told after 2 days of filling in forms and conducting interviews that they had been successful. One last formality was to pay the recruitment company the fees for the job placement, which amounted to $6,000. The monies, they were told, were for fees, the processing of the visas that they would need. They paid the money and then everything went quiet. David tried to call the recruitment company on the number that they had given him, but the phones were dead. They'd paid the scammers $6,000. Now, speaking of scams, the UK government and a number of states in the US, in their infinite wisdom, have decided to require online verification to surf the web. A number of popular websites have already adopted this such as X, Reddit, and Trustpilot to name a few. The governments say the new laws are designed to protect young people online, but it is the start of a new mass surveillance system. Six police officers came to my house and arrested me. Why? Because I've been talking about my daughter's school on a WhatsApp group. US, a website set up to allow women to share red flag behavior on violent men was hacked and their personal data and even their physical addresses were leaked onto the internet. I mean, both of them had yachting experience, and the fact that they were able to fool them into thinking that they were legitimate is is proven by the fact that they were fooled. Even though they were only young, they were experienced, which tells you that the people who did the scam have yachting experience themselves. They also said that they did a good job of redirecting them when they had doubts. Even Even David's father was a bit skeptical about this job, speaking to David and saying, "Look, I'm not sure if this is a legitimate job. Why would they hire you from the US when there's all these people in in Europe who, you know, who they could hire without having to go through all this trouble?" But, David continued with the recruitment process and only told his father of the scam after he'd paid the money. In a bid to try and trace these scammers, David actually contacted the person on the partially redacted application form that the scammers had sent him to prove that they'd recruited others, only to find out that that person had actually been caught in the same scam. Eventually, David was able to find contact details for the yacht that they were supposedly being recruited for, only to be told by the yacht that they don't use any such companies. They do all the recruitment themselves and they had no contact with anybody about recruiting anybody for this yacht. So, how can you avoid this scam? Professional yacht and ship recruitment agencies around the world are required to be registered with the national coast guard agency or equivalent and must be MLC compliant. MLC stands for Maritime Labour Convention. The convention is known as MLC 2006, effectively becoming binding in international law and established minimum working and living standards for all seafarers on ships. Basic aims of the Maritime Labour Convention are to protect seafarers' rights, including in recruitment. MLC requirements A1.4 paragraph 5B, fees and charges, requires that no fees or other charges for seafarer recruitment or placement or for providing employment to seafarers are borne directly or indirectly, in whole or in part, by the seafarer other than the cost of the seafarer obtaining a national statutory medical certificate, the national seafarer's book, and a passport or other similar personal travel documents, not including, however, the cost of visas, which shall be borne by the ship owner. So, what does that mean? It basically means you do not have to pay the recruiters any money at all. Now, the MLC is ratified in 110 countries, including Croatia, by the way, where the scammers said they were operating from. So, it's important to check that these recruitment companies that you deal with are registered by the MLC. So, how can you check that a company is registered? There is a list of countries that are signed on to the MLC labor convention, a link of which I'll put in the description. Uh each country will have a list of registered companies such as this one. This is one from the UK. Uh for example, professional recruitment agencies in the UK would be registered with the MCA, the Maritime Coastguard Agency, and be MLC compliant. We spoke to one of these agencies, and they said the recruitment agents would need to have this certificate in date, which lasts for 5 years, and there is a 2 and 1/2 year intermediary audits carried out to ensure compliance. Now, if we if we have a quick scan of this list, you'll see some of the companies on this list, Burgess Yachts, a company that we've talked about before. They are on this list because they are UK-based company. And like I said, this this list is only for the UK, but every country will have its own list. So, what should you do? The first thing when making contact with a company, if you don't know this company, or even if you do know this company, ask them if they are registered with the MLC, and ask to see a copy of their certificate. Most companies have links to their their certificate on their website, such as this one here from Faststream, which is a UK-based uh recruitment company. You click on the link, and it all pops the certificate. Now, don't just go by that though. If you if you do go on to a website and it shows a certificate, get take that certificate, and go and look on the list for that country. If we look on the uh the UK's list, you will find Faststream on that list. That list is from the government, then we know that that's a legitimate company. Now, if they don't have one, like uh with Wilson Halligan here, they said that they can't they can provide you with a copy of the certificate if you email them at this address, something that we did. We did it without telling them who we were, and we were contacted by one of the directors from the company saying that if you check the list, you'll see that they're on that list. They didn't actually send us a copy of the certificate, actually, but um they pointed us to the list of of the MCA, and there they were. The most important thing to remember though is at any point, if they ask you to give them money, you stop right there. Do not pay them any money, and then leave them well alone because they are scammers. Another thing you could do is ask them, "Am I required to pay you any upfront fees before starting this job, assuming I'm successful?" Now, at this point, a legitimate company should tell you, "Actually, you won't pay us any fees. Uh the the fee of recruitment is borne by the ship that you're going to work on." Let's say I get recruited, and my monthly salary is 5,000 euros a month. The recruitment company will get the equivalent of my first month's pay. The reason why we made this video is because we want you to learn from the mistakes of David and Linda. I get Like I said, that's not their real names. Uh the industry is an industry of anonymity, the reason why we've hidden the names. The important thing is to get the story out there, so people understand what is required of them when they are being recruited by any company. Working in the yachting industry is one of the best jobs that I've ever had. You know, you just have to be smart, and make sure that you don't get caught in one of these scams. Like I said, if the job seems too good to be true, then it might well be just that, too good to be true, and it's actually a fake job.

- De-escalation Skills Training for Employees | Dr. Jeremy Pollack: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] hi I'm Dr Jeremy Pollock I'm an applied social psychologist and workplace conflict specialist and today I'm going to go over a powerful three-step process to deescalate 10 situations with customers co-workers or friends before I do please subscribe to this channel for more tips and techniques on conflict resolution and deescalation let's talk about deescalation when people are escalated that's angry frustrated upset they're not in a place where they can effectively resolve conflicts conflict resolution often requires creative problem solving collaboration compromise these higher order executive functions happen in a particular part of the brain an emotionally escalated person is literally operating in a different area of the brain the fear and anger centers very different regions from our creative problem solving areas so to problem solve with someone who is emotionally escalated we have to First deescalate which means we have to literally help them shift from one brain region to another so how do we do this to deescalate we first need to clearly understand what tends to escalate people the three most common sources of emotional escalation are someone feels unsafe they feel disrespected or they have a problem and they feel like the person they're talking to doesn't care so that's a real or perceived lack of safety lack of respect or lack of care when we're talking about customer deescalation techniques the last one is typically the most common source of customer escalation in other words customers get angry and upset most often because they're coming to a customer facing representative with their problem but it seems like the rep doesn't really care imagine if you had an issue with a product or a service that was really stressing you out because it was affecting your life in some greater way as is usually the case with the customers who are upset for example a web server customer can't get her website to work which affects her ability to do business an appliance customer didn't receive his new refrigerator on time and now he has no way of keeping his family's food from rotting over the weekend A Bank customer can't withdraw money from her account account which literally affects everything in her life and they go to the customer service agent with her issue but the agent responds very robotically without much compassion or empathy maybe gives a very weak apology but it's obvious the agent doesn't really care about the greater or deeper stresses in their lives this issue is causing how angry and frustrated would you feel many of us have had this experience and it's the easiest way to escalate a customer situation so if the most common source of customer escalation is a lack of perceived care then we have to help the customer feel that we do care if we want to deescalate here are a few powerful steps to deescalate customers by showing we do care about their issue and the stress it's causing number one listen and repeat whatever you hear from the customer before responding or trying to resolve the issue first repeat back what you heard them say this form of validation is an incredibly powerful way to help someone feel heard and cared for and it could immediately help an individual calm down in fact Studies have shown that empathic paraphrasing can actually regulate negative emotions in someone else and not only does repeat eating help the customer feel heard it also helps you ensure you've heard them correctly so it gives them a chance to correct you if not here's what it might sound like from a customer service rep so you ordered with expedit shipping but it hasn't gotten to you yet and now your oven isn't even functioning and you can't cook dinner for your kids or oh wow okay so your neighbor stays up all night and watches TV loudly and you can hear it through the walls again just repeat or paraphrase back what you heard now step two empathize and apologize empathy is not just a feeling you have it's a feeling that you clearly Express beh Behavior l through tone and Body Language if you're face to face then your facial expression and even hand gestures should contain emotionality and whether you're face to face or not your vocal tone should contain emotion as well this lets someone know you're there with them in the issue not simply an outsid or looking in empathy helps people feel like they have a partner or a teammate rather than feeling so alone in their problem and with customers specifically apologies go a long way but they need to be authentic they need to be hurt apologize for what they're experiencing or going through or apologize for anything you could take take responsibility for on behalf of the company just don't apologize for someone's feelings as that can sound condescending the first two parts together might sound like this okay so you ordered with expedited shipping but can't cook dinner for your kids wow my gosh I'm so sorry this is happening I really apologize for this delay I can I can only imagine how stressful this is or oh wow okay so your neighbor stays up all night and watches TV loudly and you can hear it through the walls H this is so frustrating you must be exhausted I'm so sorry using tones and words that expressed emotions related to stress frustration or aggravation can again really help the individual feel heard and understood so by now feeling heard and cared for most customers should feel at least a bit calmer than when they started and a little bit more ready to find a solution so now it's time for the final step reassure and resolve you want to reassure the individual that you're in this with them that they're not alone and that you're not leaving until you can actually resolve the issue or find someone who can so for example okay Jane I'm going to stay on with you until we find a solution that works can we take a look at your account or okay Tom I'm not the right department to get this taken care of but I'm going to stay on with you until we reach someone in the right Department I'm not going anywhere or Dan how about if I go talk to your neighbor and see if I can help and I'll get back to you by 3 p.m. whether I reach him or not to give you an update does that sound like a good first step remember to get to problem solving we first have to deescalate and that means helping someone shift out of their fight or flight centers and back into their problem solving beinging their creative regions we can do this by helping them feel safe respected and for customers especially that we care about their problem and their deeper life stresses it's causing care is the key to deescalate customers thanks CONSUMER INSOLVENCIES HAVE HIT A 17-YEAR HIGH, THE HIGHEST QUARTERLY LEVEL SINCE THE FINANCIAL CRISIS OF 2009. IN THE FIRST THREE MONTHS OF THIS YEAR, MORE THAN 37,000 CANADIANS FILED FOR INSOLVENCY TO GET RELIEF BECAUSE THEY CANNOT PAY THEIR DEBTS. THAT IS UP 8.5% FROM THE YEAR BEFORE. NISHA PATEL IS JOINING US NOW TO TALK ABOUT THESE NUMBERS. WHAT IS BEHIND THIS JUMP? >> Reporter: IT IS A PERFECT STORM, ADRIENNE. THE JOB MARKET IS SHAKY. HOUSING AND FOOD IS EXPENSIVE. AND NOW HIGH GAS PRICES ARE STRETCHING BUDGETS EVEN FURTHER. CANADIANS ARE RELYING ON DEBT TO SURVIVE. AND AS THAT DEBT BUILDS UP, IT CAN BE TOUGH TO CARRY MONTH AFTER MONTH. ALL IT TAKES IS FOR THE RENT TO BE HIKED OR YOUR CAR TO BREAK DOWN, OR WORK SHIFTS TO BE CUT TO HIT THAT BREAKING POINT WHERE SOMEONE CAN NO LONGER PAY THEIR BILLS. AND IT MAY NOT SURPRISE YOU TO HEAR THAT TWO OF THE PROVINCES WHERE THE CONSUMER INSOLVENCIES ARE SEEING THE BIGGEST INCREASES -- ONTARIO AND B.C., TWO OF THE PROVINCES THAT HAVE BEEN HIT HARDEST BY U.S. TARIFFS. AND WHERE THEIR HOUSING MARKETS ARE SEEING THE DEEPEST DOWNTURNS. >> FOR THE LAST 20 YEARS, UP TO YEAR OR TWO AGO, IF MY CREDIT CARD DEBT WAS GETTING OUT OF CONTROL, I COULD REFINANCE MY HOUSE, BECAUSE MY HOUSE PRICE WENT UP AND GET IT ON MY MORTGAGE AND PAY OFF MY UNSECURED DEBT. THAT IS NO LONGER POSSIBLE BECAUSE MY HOUSE PRICE DIDN'T GO UP IN LAST YEAR OR TWO AND IT MIGHT HAVE EVEN GONE DOWN. >> Adrienne:SO THIS PICTURE IS BRUTAL.
