  1. @magicmike7198 [19 likes]: A Watch Story. Once upon a time, there was a poor man’s watch that dreamed of being a Rolex. We called that watch Rollie. Rollie looked like a Submariner automatic chronometer black dial. But it was not. Some forked tongues called it a fake. Others, more conciliatory, called it an homage. Not feeling good with itself, Rollie just wanted to be seen as the Rolex it was modeled on. But the horological world out there can be cruel. For every person who would not judge Rollie, ten more would remind it of its more than modest nature. So one day, Rollie decided to take the bull by the horns, or the watch by the lugs, and set off on a pilgrimage to what it had heard was the Mecca of watches. A particular store located in Miami. Rumors spoke of a magical place where luxury watches came alive at night, when the doors locked. So began Rollie's long trek on the long yellow brick road to 58 SE 1st St, Miami. A trek filled with adventures which may be told one day in the appendix books. Suffice it to say that once in the Promised Land, Rollie had to make its way. The store's luxurious watches weren't particularly kind to Rollie. The Pateks, proud, beautiful and a bit haughty, looked down on it. The APs, sturdy and boorish, were even on the threatening side. The RMs, on the other hand, were in a world of their own and ignored it like they ignored everything else. The true anchor for Rollie was the Rolexes. Aside from the model called the Hulk who tended to get angry for trifles, the others Rolex models were accommodating. Probably out of pity more than anything else. But still, the one called the Batman, the true and calm leader of the pack, undertook the education of Rollie. As far as leadership goes, The Batman was taking example on Dan, one of the tenants of the place. The goal was not to transform Rollie into a Rolex, which it could never be, but rather to give it back its letters of nobility, to restore its self-confidence, to accept itself as it was and to surpass the limits that the mechanical world had given it. To give it a joie de vivre, one of the characteristics of the store, modeled on the good nature of Charlie, the future Daron of the place. Rollie was soon a transformed watch. It became quickly the darling of the store, rapidly growing on the Pateks, APs and RMs who eventually accepted it as it was. Even the Batgirl, moved by Rollie's resilience, gave in to its clumsy but courageous advances. The legendary beauty of the Batgirl was matched only by that of the store's female employees, the sublime and hypnotizing Natalie, Raysa, Sandra and Betty. This caused some tension among jealous watches but these were quickly mitigated by Carlos, the Master Jeweler of the place whose pugilistic prowess soon put every watch in their place. Proud of what it had become, which was itself, Rollie never took its place on the shelves of the store but was instead named the tenant of this little watch fellowship, some kind of a handywatch, if you will, modeling its habits on those of Big Willy and Silent Ahmed, two dear employees of the premises. Thus ended Rollie's existential quest of self-discovery, of self-purpose. From an altogether banal watch, uncomfortable in its bracelet, it had found a raison d'être. From one watch supposedly imitating another, it had become its own object. Respected by its peers, loved by the Queen of the place. Such is one of the legends of the store affectionately known as CRM Jewelers. Late at night, it is said that if you pass near the store, you can see Rollie and the watches come alive and party. Not on Friday evenings, mind you. For that is the day of the holy broadcast, the day of communion. The day when CRM transmits the Good News to the rest of the planet. But who knows? Should you have the opportunity to enter the premises, during the day, perhaps you could catch a glimpse, if only for a moment, of Rollie's shadow on the various watch displays, but that's unlikely. Should you ever see a faint redness on the components of the Batgirl, you'd know then Rollie was close by. But that remains yet to be seen. Unlike the Hunchback of Notre-Dame, or the Phantom of the Opera, whose stories are sadder, Rollie strolls through the hidden lairs of the store, watching discreetly and proudly over its watch friends until the wee hours of the night when the real party begins. When all the watches act like everyday was Friday. Like everyday was CRM day.

  15. @devurandom: Seizing assets based on one's nationality is an evil practice; especially, since the wealthy or even ordinary citizens do not have anything to do with a totalitarian regime's War policies.  The Nazis looted and seized everything from the Jewish people simply because they were wealthy and intelligent.  Hitler needed gold and money for his War Machine.  Should the West adopt such an evil practice now because Ukraine needs money for its War Machine.  Let's not forget that it takes two to tango.  Zelenskyy had diplomatic cards he could play but chose not to.  He could have stepped down voluntarily "under duress", then pursued sanctions against any future puppet president and sanctions against Russia's interference in his country political affairs.  He would have saved his country hundreds of billions of dollars in damages, as well as he would have saved the lives of every man, woman, and child that has been killed since.  
Making sport over seized assets should be illegal because of something known as a "Gdfather Clause" in English common law.  People that have utilized their own money or resources, in a transaction presumably made under good faith which is free and clear of any encumbrance whatsoever during the time of transaction cannot be imposed additional measures of performance once a particular transaction has been legally finalized.  If for whatever reason any particular transaction or sale of goods, products, or services becomes illegal in the future, it does not negate or change the fact that the previous transaction had already occurred while such transaction was still legal.  Under the UCC regulations if a transaction has already occurred in the good faith and payment has been received for any product, good, or service and said transaction concluded satisfactory to both the buyer and the seller, the transaction cannot be imposed additional fees or further requirements of performance if no such promises, warranties, or restrictions were in the bill of sale.  
What does all this mean: 
if truly the assets themselves were the product of transactions of an illegal venture, then the due diligence of the contracting party who received the money to build the yacht would be the one on the hook to repay the the money to the requesting government. The yacht itself would be "third party" to the transaction and the due diligence of the accepting party would be the one responsible for supposedly accepting corrupt oligarch money.  The Yatch is free and clear of any performance measure made after the fact.  
The yacht building Company, on the other hand, is on the hook for accepting money that it should have otherwise known came from questionable sources.  
What America has sought to enforce upon the world is their version of "civil forfeiture", which is rampant with abuse.  Ordinary Americans, even disabled veterans, are having their money taken from them simply because they were driving with it.  
There's poison in the Kool-Aid the media is giving Joe Plumber, so make sure you understand how these policies affect you at home.  Anyone that does a simple youtube search on "civil forfeiture abuse" will know how bad it is.  The rest of the world does not need to adopt these "nazi-style" property takeover practices.

- This Is Why Yachts Are Suddenly CHEAP – Prices Are CRASHING: Kind: captions Language: en Right now, yachts that would have sparked bidding wars 3 years ago are just sitting. Dealers are discounting new boats 20-30%. Banks are dumping nearly new wake boats and motor yachts at auction. And at the very top of the market, governments are literally fire selling seized super yachts for half of what they were worth on paper. So, what happened? Why are yachts suddenly cheap? And why are prices still falling? In this video, we're going to unpack the great recalibration of the yacht market. How the COVID boom created a fake reality, what changed with interest rates, insurance, and inventory, which types of boats are getting crushed the hardest, and how repossessions and distress sales are quietly resetting prices everywhere. And if you stick with me till the end, I'll give you a simple four-step buyers playbook you can use right now to spot genuine opportunities without getting wrecked by hidden costs. If you like honest, no-BS breakdowns of the boating world, hit subscribe and tap the bell. It tells me to keep making these deep dives for you. All right, let's start with the big picture. The COVID sugar high and the hangover. From 2020 to 2022, the yacht market went completely off script. Travel shut down, work from home exploded, money was cheap, and suddenly let's buy a boat went from crazy idea to logical family plan. Dealers ran out of stock. People were wiring deposits on boats they had never seen, sometimes waving surveys, sometimes paying 20, 30, even 40% more than pre-COVID prices. Anything that floated sold. Pontoons, wake boats, center consoles, small cruisers, big cruisers, even tired old sailboats that had been baking in boat yards for years suddenly had buyers. At the same time, manufacturers were dealing with supply chain chaos. Engines were delayed, resin was delayed, electronics were delayed. So, yards did what every industry did. They ramped up orders. Dealers double ordered to make sure they got something to sell. Builders expanded capacity, hired workers, opened shifts. That's important because boat building has a long lead time. The boats dealers are trying to sell in 2024 and 2025 were spec'd, financed, and built based on the insane demand of 2021. Now, fast forward. Travel is open again. Offices want people back. Groceries, rent, fuel, and mortgages are all more expensive. The COVID cohort of first-time owners has discovered that boats are not just a big purchase, they're a lifestyle and a constant bill. So, they're trying to sell at the exact same time that all those extra boats manufacturers built during the boom are finally hitting dealer lots. Demand down, supply up. That's the core of why prices are crashing. But, there's another piece that really drives the knife in, the cost of money. The end of cheap capital. Most boats under, say, 50 ft are not paid for in cash, they're financed. During the boom, you could get a boat loan around 4% interest if your credit was decent. Monthly payments felt manageable, especially with stock portfolios up and real estate booming. Now, those same boat loans are coming back at 7, 8, 9% and higher. So, imagine a $200,000 boat. At 4% over 15 years, the payment might land somewhere around $1,500 a month. Painful, but doable for a dual-income family that thinks they'll use the boat every weekend. At 8 or 9% that payment jumps into the 2,000-plus range. And that's before dockage, insurance, fuel, and maintenance. The same thing happens at every price tier. Super prime buyers with amazing credit see their payments jump 20 to 30%. Prime buyers with normal good credit see 35 to 40% increases. Subprime buyers, a lot of them are simply locked out. The banks either won't lend at all or the rates are so high the math makes no sense. Now, zoom out to the dealerships. Dealers don't just get stock for free, they borrow money against it on something called a floor plan. That's basically a giant credit line they use to buy boats from the manufacturer. While the boat sits on the lot, they're paying interest on that floor plan loan. When interest was close to zero and boats were selling in days, nobody cared. Carrying costs were tiny. In 2025, with interest rates double or triple and boats taking an extra month or two to sell, those holding costs are brutal. So, dealers are under pressure from both sides. Fewer people can qualify or are willing to finance a boat, and every extra week that boat sits there costs the dealer money. The only way to relieve that pressure is to discount hard, sell fast, and free up credit. That's why you're seeing non-current new boats, 2023 and 2024 models, being blown out at 20-30% off just to get them off the books. And once those new boat prices fall, every used boat above them has to fall even harder. If you can walk into a showroom and buy a brand new 2024 for 30% off, you're not paying 90% of retail for a 2-year-old model from a private seller. You're going to demand 40 or 50% off. That's exactly what we're seeing. From shortage to glut, the inventory flood. So, demand is weaker, money is more expensive, and dealers are desperate to liquidate. Now, layer in the inventory story. During the boom, dealers were placing huge orders because every boat they could get their hands on was selling instantly. Manufacturers expanded production based on that demand signal, but because boats take time to build, the peak of production arrived after the peak of demand. By late 2023 and 2024, buyers had already started pulling back, but the pipeline was still full. Finished boats kept rolling off production lines and onto dealer lots into a softening market. Today, in a lot of segments, we are in outright oversupply. You see it if you walk a marina, rows of unsold new pontoons, wake boats, fishing boats, cabin cruisers, all with faded 2024 stickers and sales tags that keep getting marked down. This is the classic bullwhip effect. A small change in real consumer demand turned into a massive swing at the manufacturing level because everyone overreacted on the way up. Now, the bullwhip is snapping back. Let's talk about where it's hitting the hardest. Segment one, the wake boat and tow boat crash. If you want to see where the market got most insane, look at wake boats. Before COVID, these were already expensive toys, but between 2020 and 2022, prices went stratospheric. A fully loaded MasterCraft, Nautique, Malibu, or similar could easily push 250 to $300,000 for a 23-26 ft boat. A lot of those boats were purchased by financing-heavy buyers. High incomes, maybe a home equity line, maybe a long boat loan. The logic was, we're home anyway, the kids can't travel, let's enjoy the lake. Now, reality has landed. Those boats have giant monthly payments, high insurance, big storage costs in the off-season, and they drink fuel. With commuting back, kids' schedules back to normal, and money tighter, a huge number of those owners are trying to get out. Retail sales for new wake boats have fallen sharply. And on the repossession side, auction lists are full of late-model tow boats with bank names in the seller field. When you see 2021 and 2022 boats, still basically new, still under warranty, being sold by lenders for half of original sticker price, that rewrites the entire value curve. The next buyer looks at that auction result and thinks, why would I pay close to list when I know the bank just dumped the same boat for 50% less? That's what a popped bubble looks like. Segment two, pontoons and smaller aluminum runabouts. Next in line are pontoons and smaller aluminum boats, the classic family COVID boat. These were the entry point for thousands of first-time owners. Simple, stable, easy to use. They exploded in popularity during the pandemic because they were the cheapest way to socially distance on the water with kids and grandparents. Many of those buyers put tiny down payments on huge loans, 15 or even 20 years at peak prices. off-season, and they drink fuel. With commuting back, kids' schedules back to normal, and money tighter, a huge number of those owners are trying to get out. Retail sales for new pontoons have fallen sharply. And on the repossession side, auction lists are full of late-model tow boats with bank names in the seller field. When you see 2021 and 2022 boats, still basically new, still under warranty, being sold by lenders for half of original sticker price, that rewrites the entire value curve. If you bought a pontoon for 80,000, borrowed nearly all of it, and that boat is now worth 55 in today's market, you can't just sell it to get out. You'd have to bring 20 or 30 grand in cash to closing to pay off the bank. A lot of people can't or won't, so they let the bank take it. That's why you see one or two-year-old pontoons in repossession auctions next to tow boats. Again, every one of those sales pushes market values lower for everyone else. Segment three, mid-market cruisers and motor yachts. The same forces are hammering cabin cruisers and motor yachts in the 35 to 70-ft range. This is the classic aspiration category, express cruisers, flybridge boats, Euro style motor yachts. In the boom, these sold to entrepreneurs, executives, and upgraders who moved out of smaller bow riders and cruisers. They bought with the expectation that yachting would be their new lifestyle and that the strong economy would continue to fund it. They've now run into three big walls. First, the same interest rate shock. A half-million-dollar boat at three or four percent interest is lifestyle stretching. At eight or nine percent plus higher fuel and dockage, it's a financial anchor. Second, the operational cost squeeze. Marinas are raising rates, diesel is not cheap, and maintenance and refit costs have exploded with inflation. Third, many of these boats require professional help to run. Once you cross into the 50 to 70-ft range, insurers and ports often want to see a licensed captain. That means crew salaries on top of everything else. So, you're seeing 50 to 70-footers show up in distressed inventory, too. Bank-owned Sunseekers, Azimuts, large American cruisers. Not junk, nice boats, relatively modern, but owned by people whose financial plan assumed the low rate, low inflation world of 2021 would last forever. It didn't. Segment four, super yachts and geopolitics. You might think the ultra-rich are immune to all this. To some degree, that's true. But, even the super yacht world is feeling the recalibration. Two big forces are at work here. First, sanctions. Russian ultra-wealthy buyers used to be a huge part of the 60-m plus market. Post-Ukraine, a lot of those yachts were frozen, arrested, or effectively taken out of normal circulation. Governments stuck with seized yachts have to deal with crew salaries, fuel, dockage, legal risk. The easy solution, sell them. And because the state just wants them gone, those forced sales often happen at deep discounts versus what brokers would have asked in a normal private sale. Think of an 80-m yacht valued around 80 million selling closer to 40, or a 70-plus meter yacht going at auction for well under replacement cost. Those numbers set psychological anchor points. A buyer looking at another 80-m will say, "Why should I pay 70 when that one just sold for 40?" Second, the new build order book is no longer crazy. In 2021, some shipyards were booked out for years. Speculative buyers would order a yacht just to flip it on delivery. Now, with interest rates up and global growth wobbling, that flip game is dead. Yards still have work, especially the very top northern European brands, but lower-tier builders and semi-custom yards are under pressure. On the brokerage side, listings are sitting longer. Price reductions in the five to 10% range on very large yachts translate to tens of millions of dollars in value simply evaporating. It's a softer, slower crash than in the tow boat world, but it's still a reset. Owners who want out quickly either accept lower offers or eat very large operating costs while they wait. The silent killer, operating costs. So far, we've talked about purchase and finance, but one of the biggest drivers pushing yachts onto the market is simply the rising cost of keeping them. Let's start with insurance. After several nasty hurricane seasons and a string of big marine claims, insurers have raised rates and tightened standards dramatically. If your boat lives in Florida, the Gulf, the Caribbean, anywhere that looks risky on a hurricane map, your premium hits are huge. In some areas, major carriers have simply pulled back or non-renewed whole categories of boats. On top of that, many insurers have introduced hard age limits. Over 20 or 25 years for smaller boats, over 30 or 40 years for larger ones, coverage goes from expensive but doable to liability only if you're lucky. But, here's the catch. Banks require full hull coverage to write or maintain a loan. Marinas often require proof of insurance even just for a slip or mooring. So, a boat that becomes uninsurable is, in practical terms, unsellable to the average financed buyer. It's not that the hull suddenly got worse. It's that the financial plumbing around it shut off. That destroys resale value overnight. Now, add crew. In the super yacht and larger motor yacht world, crew salaries have climbed sharply. There's a limited pool of qualified captains, engineers, and stews, and more yachts competing for them. Monthly payroll for a 40-m yacht crew can easily rival a nice house's mortgage payment all by itself. Finally, maintenance and refit inflation. Parts are more expensive, yard labor is more expensive. Supply chain delays mean boats sit half disassembled on the hard while invoices roll in. Simple annual services cost 20, 30, even 40% more than they did three or four years ago. Put all that together, and many owners have arrived at the same uncomfortable conclusion. I like this boat, but it's not worth what it costs me every year to keep it. And when enough people reach that point at once, a wave of for sale signs hits the docks. The repossession and auction wave. Some owners can sell before the math gets too ugly. Others can't. When a boat's market value drops faster than the loan balance is paid down, you end up with negative equity. If you get sick of paying, or your income drops, or your business hits a rough patch, you're stuck. If you try to sell, and the best offer is 50,000 less than what you owe the bank, you either find 50,000 cash or you walk. That's where repossessions and voluntary surrenders come in. Lenders don't want to be in the boat business, but they hate non-performing loans more. So, they take the boat back, hand it to a liquidator or auction house, and clear it fast at whatever the wholesale market will bear. To you and me, those auction results might just look like good deals. To the rest of the market, they are price benchmarks. If a like-new two-year-old tow boat sells at auction for 100,000, that's now the real value. The dealer up the road is not getting 200 for the same boat. The private seller with an optimistic listing at 180, good luck. This is how distress in a relatively small part of the market, the repo yard, drags down values everywhere. Regional pressure points. This recalibration is global, but it's not evenly distributed. North America, and especially Florida, is the epicenter. Financing-heavy culture plus aggressive interest rate hikes plus a brutal insurance environment equals fast price moves. You see it in the volume of listings, in the size of discounts, and in the number of price-reduced tags on brokerage sites. On the US West Coast and in the Northeast, environmental rules, limited slip supply, and high costs of living make ownership expensive even in good times. In a downturn, that pushes marginal owners to sell quickly rather than hold on. Europe is softer but similar. Economic worries in Germany, higher energy costs, and the loss of Russian buyers for big med yachts all weigh on prices. Operational costs there, VAT, marina fees, refits, are not exactly low, either. One interesting twist is charter. As the cost and complexity of owning climbs, more people are deciding to charter instead of buy. That supports the value of boats that are commercial-ready, lots of cabins, built to code, good charter reputations, and hurts the value of yachts that are expensive to run but don't generate income. Over time, we're likely to see a clearer split between private toys and commercial assets with very different value trajectories. So, is this a crash or just a reset? In some ways, what we're seeing is simply reality reasserting itself. For about three years, boats behaved like speculative assets. People bought them expecting to sell later for as much or more. In some cases, they actually did. You could order a boat, wait for delivery, and flip it for a profit because somebody was willing to pay anything to get one right now. That was never sustainable. Historically, boats are depreciating assets. You buy them because you value the time and the experience, not because you think you'll make money. The crash feeling comes from moving from that fantasy back to normal depreciation very quickly. And from doing it in a world where the ongoing costs of ownership are shooting higher at the same time. So, what do you do with all this as a buyer? Let's finish with the promised playbook. A four-step buyer's playbook for the crash. If you want to take advantage of this reset without getting crushed, here are four rules. Rule one, buy the whole cost, not just the price. The sticker or asking price is the down payment on the lifestyle, not the total. Before you get excited about a big discount, write down annual slip or storage cost where you live, realistic insurance premiums for that size and age, fuel at the hours you'll actually use, basic annual maintenance, and a refit allowance. If that yearly number doesn't fit your life comfortably, walk away, even if the boat itself looks like a screaming bargain. Rule two. Check the three financial choke points, insurance, financing, and mortgage, before you fall in love. Call an insurer and ask whether they will write full coverage on the year, make, and size you're looking at in your region. If the answer is no or liability only, that boat is cheap for a reason. Talk to your lender before you shop, so you know how much the payment changes at different rates and terms. And make sure you actually have somewhere to put the boat. In many areas, slips are scarce. There's no point buying a 50-footer if you can't find a berth for it. Rule three. Respect the distress market. Before you commit, look at repossession and auction results for similar boats. That tells you the real wholesale floor. If a bank just sold a 3-year-old example of the boat you want for $100,000 at auction, paying 180 in a private sale is generous. You might still choose to pay more for a better-kept boat with full records, but at least you'll know where the bottom truly is. Rule four. Prefer simple, liquid boats over complex, niche toys. In a tough market, versatility holds value. A well-kept center console, a simple express cruiser, a solid cruising boat, or a yacht that charters well is easier to resell than a hyper-specialized towboat or a very complex 60-footer loaded with finicky systems. If you're not sure how long you'll want to own, pick a boat that appeals to the broadest possible next buyer. Wrapping up. Yachts are suddenly cheap, not because boating is dying, but because the fantasy economics of the pandemic years have collided with the reality of higher interest rates, higher running costs, and a huge oversupply of boats. For sellers, this is painful. For buyers with cash, patience, and a clear head, it's a generational opportunity. If you focus on total cost of ownership and avoid the traps, uninsurable hulls, negative equity toys, and boats that only made sense in a world of free money. If you found this useful, hit subscribe and turn on notifications so you don't miss the next deep dive. I'll keep trading hype for honest analysis and hopefully help you make smart moves on the water instead of expensive mistakes.

- How Maritime Law Works: Kind: captions Language: en I love making interesting videos. The most interesting topics are often exceptions—deviations from the norm. All of us live in countries, where there are laws and rules and governing bodies telling us what we can and can’t do. But, 70% of the world is ocean, where there are no countries—no governing bodies to tell us what’s right and wrong. That’s why maritime law exists. Let’s start with a hypothetical: a baby is born on a cruise ship sailing in the middle of the Atlantic ocean. What nationality does it take? This is the coast of some fictional place in some fictional country governed by some fictional government. From this line, which is the water line at the lowest low tide, every country is allowed 12 miles of territorial waters. It used to be 3 miles—the distance a cannon could shoot off shore—but that has since changed. Those twelve miles are the property of a country. They can do pretty much whatever they please in it and all domestic laws apply. Foreign ships are, however, sometimes allowed to enter into these waters under the principle of innocent passage. If ships have an innocent purpose—which does not include fishing, polluting, weapons practice, or spying—they are allowed to pass through territorial waters of a foreign nation without permission as long as they do so quickly and without stopping on shore. Beyond the territorial waters there is another 12 miles of the contiguous zone. This zone allows a country to enforce laws as long as they fall into one of four categories. If the laws have to do with customs, taxation, immigration, or pollution, they can be enforced in the contiguous zone. Beyond the contiguous waters is the Exclusive Economic Zone, also known as the EEZ. This zone extends 200 nautical miles from shore. Beyond the territorial waters the EEZ is in international waters, however, only the country who holds the exclusive economic zone has the right to harvest natural resources in this area. This law was originally set up to help with disputes over fishing rights but has since been incredibly useful with the boom in oil drilling. All these laws do, however, occasionally cause some disputes due to overlapping zones. This is the South China Sea—an incredibly important waterway. Nearly 1/3rd of the world’s shipping traffic passes through it and it reportedly has huge untapped oil reserves. China has this land so it says it has all this water, Malaysia has this land so it says it has all this water, Vietnam has this land so it says it has all this water, Brunei has this land so it says it has all this water, the Philippines has this land so it says it has all this water, and Taiwan has this land so it says it has all this water. When two countries are less than 400 nautical miles away from each other, it is up to them to decide where their respective economic zones end. Most solve it civilly by separating the zones at the equidistant point from each of their shores, however, when the stakes are so high, such as in the South China Sea, countries can be a bit less cordial. So, our cruise ship baby. Let’s change the hypothetical and say that the cruise ship was sailing in US territorial waters—less than 12 miles away from shore. Every oceangoing vessel is required to be registered in some country. You’ll notice that most large cruise ships are registered in tiny far-away countries. Panama, a nation with fewer people that Minneapolis, holds the registration of one quarter of the world’s ships because taxes and labor costs are low. When a ship is in international waters, the laws of the country of registration apply. A ship registered in Amsterdam could legally have prostitution and marijuana on board, as long as they got rid of the drugs and shut down the brothels before sailing into territorial waters. Once a ship is in the territorial waters of a country, the onboard laws switch to that of the country the ship is physically in. This is the same for nationality law, kinda. A baby born on a Dutch ship within 12 miles of the US is a baby born in America. Since the US is one of the 30 countries that unconditionally grants citizenship to any baby born within the country, a baby born in US territorial waters is lucky enough to receive the world’s 8th most powerful passport. There are two exceptions to this rule. Foreign Diplomats visiting or living in the US with a diplomatic passport are not subject to the laws of the US or any other nation other than their own. Consequently, the babies of foreign diplomats do not automatically receive American citizenship. Additionally, the babies of individuals staging a hostile invasion or occupation of American territory are not granted American citizenship upon birth. Here’s where things get even more confusing. Even though a ship in international waters is an extension of the territory of the nation it’s registered in law wise, the rules for nationality are different. The United Nations Treaty on the Reduction of Statelessness, which is followed by… some… countries, says that a baby born in international waters should just take the nationality of their parents. Most of the world’s countries use the principle of bloodline to determine if a baby should get citizenship rather than whether or not a baby was born in the country. However, there are some countries that won’t give citizenship to a baby born outside the country. In that case, the baby would take the citizenship of the country in which the ship was registered. Alright, that’s enough with babies. There’s a long history of exploiting maritime laws. During prohibition, US ships started to change their registration to Panama and other foreign countries so they could serve alcohol in international In the mid-century, casino boats left from many cities where gambling was illegal to partake in legal gambling in international In 2005, entrepreneur Roger Green started SeaCode, a company that planned to evade US labor laws by placing an old cruise ship 12 miles off the shore of California. They would bring in foreign coders and house them in this ship where they would not have to abide by US wage laws or go through the difficult visa application process. The idea never came to fruition but the technical legality of it just shows how convoluted maritime law is. The laws for airplanes are pretty much the same. Technically, once an airplane has taken off, the laws of the country of registration apply. The only law that is applies and differs among countries is the drinking age. A British Airways flight from New York to London can serve alcohol to 18 year olds, however, in most cases, airlines choose to follow the laws of the origin country. Spacecraft also follow very similar laws, and luckily, I have a whole other video just about Space Law. Make sure to check it out here. You can also click here to subscribe to Wendover Productions and follow me on Twitter @WendoverPro. Please also be sure to watch my last video on Why College is so Expensive. It’s a great video so please check it out if you haven’t done so already. Thank you for watching and I’ll see you soon with another Wendover Productions video.

Maritime laws have ancient origins, with the earliest formal codes established by the island of Rhodes around 900 BC, which influenced later Roman law. Over time, various cultures, including the Egyptians, Greeks, and later the English, contributed to the development of these laws, leading to the modern system of maritime law we have today.

- Maritime search and rescue - Documentary: Kind: captions Language: en Mayday, mayday, mayday, mayday. These are the most dangerous waters in the world. This is a French fishing trawler. The water is getting more and more dangerous. Constant streams of tankers sail by. There are plenty of rocks and gusts from the storm that could sink any ship afloat. There's a boat that's sinking. We're sinking. We're sinking. These waters, respected and feared by sailors around the world, are off the coast of Brittany in northwest France. They're called the passage of Ushant. Each year, shipwrecks claim the lives of dozens on board, and ecological catastrophes have happened here all too often. During stormy weather, lifeboat crews are on 24-hour standby, professionals or just volunteers ready to save the lives of others, even if it means risking their own. Winters are their busiest time. In the Port of Brest and the crew of the Abeille Bourbon is monitoring weather reports. On board, the Abeille's captain Charles Claden, nicknamed Carlos, is getting ready to cast off. He's a legend in this town of seafarers, where over the past 20 years he's been involved with or led most of the major local rescue operations. As soon as the winds reach 40 kilometers an hour, the authorities send us out. By then, there are storm warnings, so we might take up position close to the sea lanes. Now we're off towards Ushant, close to where there might be any trouble. Carlos steers into position exactly at the entrance to the straits. Warning, strong gusts of wind and heavy-- to very heavy seas. Heavy swells from the west are expected tomorrow night off the Cape of Brittany in the Atlantic. Close to Ushant, the first gusts are being felt. As he heads further out to sea, Carlos's vessel passes an increasing number of local boats sailing in the opposite direction. Sailors here know it's unwise to risk the open seas in stormy weather. Fishermen and yachtsmen alike are all heading for shelter. After a one-hour journey, the Abeille arrives off the island of Ushant in the Bay of Stiff, well protected by a natural rock barrier. Here, the tugboat is in its element at the entrance to the straits, ready to aid those in distress at a moment's notice. Over the past 20 years, Carlos has come to know the waters of Ushant well. There are some very strong currents. There's mist and there's rocks scattered just about everywhere around, and there are a lot of vessels that use these waters, merchant ships, of course. We weigh up all the factors and elements each time a ship gets close. On average, there are about 150 vessels that come through here a day. In the Cotentin area, there are 250 to 300 ships a day, and over in the [inaudible 00:04:36] appear as many as 500 a day. So the more ships there are, the more chance of a collision. In order to avoid collisions, the ships follow strict maritime traffic regulations. The boats heading west are in the red lane, and those heading east are in the green lanes, so-called Ushant Sea lanes, and within the zone, ships are expressly forbidden to cross lanes. Outside the zone, it's a different story, however, and it's virtually a free for all. All sorts of vessels vie for the narrow passage, including the frequent channel ferries that cross to and fro between France and England, and the large number of fishing boats that cast their nets amidst the tankers, enormous tankers that bear down on the sea lanes with apparent disregard to the danger, container ships and tankers, both transporting the chemicals and fuels that Europe needs. Monsters such as this, more than 200 metres long and laden with as much freight as can be carried by 8,000 trucks. They need at least ten kilometers to come to a halt. From the bridge, visibility is zero. These huge vessels are often the cause of some of the worst disasters at sea. At the helicopter base at Lanveoc near Brest, rescue crews are on standby and as the weather continues to close in, know they might be called into action at any time. The weather as well, let's say it's [inaudible 00:06:30]. The seas are now very heavy, so we'll soon see what will happen in this area. Didier Gusmini, also known as Gus, is ready to take off. What concerns him most is the cumulus nimbus clouds, which are a threat to the helicopter pilots. Some of them which are, well, let's say well developed, can smash a plane in two. They're extremely violent and there's a lot of ice locked up in those clouds which could freeze an airplane. It's exactly what you don't want if you're flying. Of course, inside there's also lightning, electrical interference, every kind of weather phenomena you could come across in this sort of cloud. The storm is increasing in intensity. He's been plying the waters off Cape Finisterre for lobster and fish for more than 25 years. He started going to sea at age 14. He is now what is known as a fishing boss. The boat belongs to him and he has a crew of two. On board, there is plenty of potentially dangerous equipment. It's a dangerous job. We spend, I don't know, 75% of our time on water. The majority of the French people work 35 hours a week. We can do 35 hours in just two days sometimes. Hard work and sacrifice since fishing has to pay to maintain the boat and feed Thiery's family. At sea, the saying goes, life gets harder and harder for the fishermen. Thiery and his small crew often work two days and two nights non-stop, without rest or sleep, always on their feet. We do work really long hours sometimes, so long that the body says, "Stop." I've known one guy who suffered from a broken bone, a fracture caused entirely by tiredness. He was so tired that when he fell just ten centimeters, he broke a bone, just from being tired. There's other things too, like your back. Our backs are strained during the sort of work, so there are lots of sailors who have back problems. Plus, we're always wet, always under stress. So our bodies get run down after a while. Fishermen are often the victims of accidents or worse at sea. Ports across Brittany routinely witness incidents, and many families have lost loved ones. This is Guilvinec, one of the largest fishing ports in Brittany. Over the years, countless fishermen or sailors from this port have been lost at sea. Marguerite's husband perished in 1973. Time, however, has not fully healed the pain of loss. Within a few minutes of the accident, I had everyone coming by to see me, the mayor and all sorts of people to come and tell me all about it. What time was it in fact? It was 10:20 in the morning, precisely. I look at that quite often. It's still painful to me. Marguerite's husband was a fisherman on board the Dany Gerard in 1973, when it was sunk by a Soviet cargo vessel. It disappeared with all hands. The worst thing is when the bodies are never found. When they find something, then you know that it's him. Otherwise, you always ask yourself, the children were always saying that he might come back someday, something which I didn't really... Albert Marguerite had been married for ten years with two small boys. The oldest boy is now also a sailor, something that Marguerite dreaded. I tried my best to stop him from going to sea. I tried everything. Everything. I even went into a depression, I swear it, to try and make him change his mind. I saw all the experts and doctors, but no, it was the only thing he wanted to do. With her son at sea, Marguerite began living with fear once again. I never wanted to listen to the radio again. It's like it's all happening over again. Every time there's a boat that sinks? Oh yes, every time, it's the same. I get goosebumps, and it's the same feelings all over again. You always hope it'll be the last one, but unfortunately it never will be. You see there's still plenty of room on the memorial. The fishing trawler Bugaled Breizh with a crew of five on board went missing on the 15th of January 2004. Debris from the ship was discovered a few weeks later, but the bodies of those missing have never been found. Human error, collision, everyone has their own theory. Some say a submarine dragged the boat down. Despite an inquest, the disappearance has never been satisfactorily explained. More recently, another fishing boat, the Klein Familie, sank off Cherbourg. The port was stunned by the news. Just a few hours after the news of the disappearance, the father of the captain loses all hope. I'm waiting for them to find my son. After that, well, I'm sure it was struck by another vessel. It couldn't be anything else. There are no rocks down there. Well, I'm really cross for me and for the others, because these stupid things will keep happening. So other ships will come along. It'll always be like that. It's just the way it is. Of the crew of five, just one will survive. The survivor, almost by miracle, is Jean Michel Gueno, the youngest crew member. For as long as he could remember, Jean Michel had wanted to become a sailor. Since the incident, he's never set foot on a boat again. This is where we would dock, right here. He's never spoken of what happened until now. How long were you on board the vessel? Well, I worked on board for nine months. We were five lads and the crew, and we'd head out to sea every day, just about. We would leave with the tide. Whatever time the tide was, we'd set sail, day or night. Then when I was having a nap, I was woken up by a huge bang and I was thrown out of the berth. Then I'm not really sure what happened next. I suppose I managed to swim because I remember I was under water and then I reached the surface somehow. There, I only saw one other lad. I don't know what. The others probably were unconscious. I don't know. I couldn't hear them, but I could hear one guy. It was Oliver. I tried to reach him, but I couldn't. He was yelling and hanging onto a float. He was drifting further and further away. So I decided to try and swim out to him. I jumped back into the sea and tried as hard as I could to swim out to where he was, but I couldn't catch up with him. At that point, I went back to the life raft and got back in. I knew he was in danger, but soon afterwards there was nothing. I couldn't hear him anymore. There was no yelling. Oliver and the rest of the crew were never found. From the moment I was in the life raft, I began thinking about God and stuff like that. All at once, I found religion. I was repeating, "My God, my God," things like that, the whole time. I was alone, just waiting to die, thinking, "Why didn't I go down with the boat?" I should have, I was thinking. Do you think if a cargo vessel hits you, don't you think they'd have realized, they would have felt something? They must have, because if the Klein Familie was a small boat, it was still about tons. So you'd think they would realize. Yes, they'd have felt something. They should have stopped at least. Does that make you angry? I was cross because when you see it sailing on, you think, well, it should stop and check, see what happened, try and help or something. You think my friend might have been saved. Then you just see it carrying on, leaving. How big was it? What kind of cargo vessel was it? It was big. It was huge. When you're in the water anyway, it feels like you're tiny, minuscule. Compared to us anyway, it was huge. How far away was it when you saw it? It was only about 20 meters away from where I was. It was plowing straight ahead on its course. Nothing was going to stop it. It was just going straight on. Yes, it was like we are the ones that had to get out of the way. The guilty ship is assumed to be the Sichem Pandora, a freighter flying the Maltese colors. On its bow, investigators discovered traces of paint that match those of the Klein Familie. The wreck of the trawler has been filmed at the bottom of the sea, but cannot be shown due to the legal proceedings that are underway. The lawyer for the Klein Familie is one of the few to have seen the video. What you see is a ship that's been completely ripped apart, a boat that's been violently struck, the stem that's still in one piece, which means the Klein Familie didn't hit another vessel, but indeed the very opposite happened. The bridge is totally destroyed, but the signal lights, buoys and the fishing gear used by the crew are still intact. So three days after the collision, some of the material on board was still functioning. This proves the Klein Familie was perfectly visible to other shipping. If the ship that crashed into the trawler had taken the usual precautions and had a proper watch, this would never have happened. The incident is the subject of legal proceedings in Malta where the freighter was registered. Until the trial begins, the Sichem Pandora is still at liberty. Every time a fishing boat returns to port, the families of the crew breathe a sigh of relief, at least until it set sails again. Marguerite was always anxious to see her husband return safely. You used to come and wait for him every time? Always, all the families, including the kids, when they weren't at school, we all used to come out to the docks and wait for Dad. The Laura is coming back to port today after two weeks at sea, and the wives have gathered at the dockside. It was The Laura that had called in the rescue services airlift its injured sailor. When they leave, it's awful, but when they return, it's wonderful. I mean, after all, they have been away for two weeks. If it was just two or three days, I would be fine, but two weeks is a long time and we worry the whole time they're away. It's hard, very hard for the wives and for the men who are out at sea. It's hard for everyone, especially when the weather's bad, and it's absolutely awful. Hi, Rambemy. How are you? How was it? Nasty weather. Very cold. Still, the sun's shining now. How is Serge doing? Well, it'll be another month or so before he's up and about, so he'd have been out of action for three or four months altogether. Well, on a two-week long trip, anything might happen. There were things that break down, don't work. Someone who gets hurt is less common, but it happens unfortunately. You've just got to keep going and deal with it. There's a lot of stress. That's why we always look forward to returning home to our wives. We hope they can help us relax, but it's not always the case. Yes, but you're at home for such a short time that we can't really take care of you how we'd like to. You sort of lose the habit. Time on shore is always brief. A few days later, another accident means the rescue services are on full alert. At 3:00 in the morning on the 31st of January, the Ece, a freighter transporting chemicals, is struck by another ship. The 22 strong crew is airlifted to safety by British helicopters stationed in the Channel Islands. This time, no one is hurt. In the Ece's hold is 10,000 tonnes of phosphoric acid. A potential environmental catastrophe looms. Early morning and Didier Gusmini and his men are deployed to the deserted vessel. We're going to drop some experts on board to evaluate the dangers. The chemical is extremely dangerous. There's a risk of pollution then? Oh yes, definitely a big risk. Carlos is part of the rescue mission. The captain of the Abeille is called upon for his technical expertise to determine whether The Ece can be towed back to port and therefore avoid a catastrophe. You just don't know. The freighter could sink within the hour or keep floating for another two weeks. We need to find out exactly what the situation is and to see what measures need to be taken. The divers will investigate how bad the breach in the hull is and see what can be done, and especially to check whether the chemicals on board have begun leaking into sea. A 30-minute flight and the helicopter is over the ship. At first sight, there's no sign of any leaks. The acid seems to be still in the hold, but the ship is listing badly. It doesn't look good, does it? It must have taken real whack. Bloody hell, it doesn't look good at all. A few hundred meters away is the General Grot-Rowecki, a 200-meter-long Maltese freighter that is believed to have collided with the Ece in the middle of the night. The Argonauts, a high seas tug arrives on the scene. It's equipped with underwater cameras that will examine the ship's hull to determine the risk of pollution and for evidence of the ship that ran into it. French Navy divers film the ship's hull, and it's an awesome sight. Ece has been eviscerated. Towards the stern, the hold has been partially breached and acid is slowly leaking out. situation is critical. With nightfall approaching, the ship runs the risk of being struck a second time by other shipping in the area. Are you ready? Cables are launched as the Abeille attempts to make fast The Ece and tow it to shore. All seems to be proceeding well, but it's still a long way to the port of Cherbourg. 2:00 in the morning, and Stefan and his crew are woken to be informed about another ship that is maneuvering close to The Ece and appears to be ignoring warnings issued by the authorities. We're off. They need to scramble. The latest news is extremely worrying. The ship continues to zigzag through the shipping lanes. It's changed its course now by 3:50. What's its latest position? Do you have it? As of 2:30? Yes. The Ece is not the only boat in danger. The ferries that connect France and England crisscross the section of the channel. On board, hundreds of unsuspecting passengers. A collision with the errant ship could be disastrous. The rescue mission has to locate it as quickly as possible. Reducing altitude, 130 feet, 55 knots, leveling out at 100 feet. The vessel is soon spotted. It appears to be a Dutch fishing boat. Its coordinates are passed on to the control centre on the mainland. It's a small fishing trawler. I'm somewhat astonished to see what it's doing. I can see no one on board. Anybody seen anybody down there? There's no one on the bridge, no sign of life, and the boat's name is barely visible on the hull. The crew might be asleep. Stefan tries to rouse them on the radio. [foreign language 00:54:20]. Still no answer. [foreign language 00:54:30]. No answer. So the powerful spotlight is directed into the boat's cabin. [foreign language 00:54:46]. I guess the person on board wasn't too concerned radio, so he kept communications to a minimum and probably ignored it. That wouldn't surprise me. Is that it then, as far as the boat's concerned? Well, pretty much in this case, we know its name, and what it's doing. We know it's a fishing boat. We'll make some inquiries, who it is, where it's from, and what it's up to. This time the boat won't be sanctioned, but any future incidents in French waters and it could be detained. At sunrise, the Abeille, which had been towing The Ece towards Cherbourg, is on its own. The freighter has sunk overnight. Well, last night at about 10:00, the ship began really listing badly, and at that point, we knew it was all over. It lay on its side and stayed afloat until about 3:30 or so this morning, and then it finally sank at 3:38. The Ece is now 70 meters below the surface with its dangerous chemical cargo threatening to leak out of its hold. Both British and French authorities monitor the area constantly. We're checking that there's been no spill, no leaks and therefore no pollution. So the ships, helicopters and planes, either English or French, are going to be patrolling the area to check on pollution. If there is, we'll need to go in. So you can tell just by looking if there's pollution? Yes, indeed. Any hydrocarbons or pollutant will form a slick on the surface and it's easily visible from air. So far, there's no slick, but the acid could leak out slowly. The Argonaut will be carefully monitoring and testing the waters. The chemist is at the exit. It's navy chemist who will lead the operation. Water from the area where the ship went down is collected in the tubes and analyzed on the spot. So far, the tests are negative, and for now there's no pollution. The surface area around the wreck remains under surveillance to this day. At Courson, near Brest, the men of the CROS, the Regional Rescue Operations Center, monitor potential polluters around clock on radar screens. Each dot represents a ship. Some ships are highlighted in green, others in orange. The orange ones are the ones that are transporting dangerous material. This could include coal, chemical products, gas, petrol. But on this morning, it's a distress signal from a pleasure boat that focuses their attention. What does the boat look like? Small cabin. Okay, small cabin. No rudder controls. Six meters long. Color? We'll take care of it, but stay close to the phone. We might need to get in touch with you again. The CROS messages all boats in the area where the boat was last seen. We are searching for an 01:00:00] class boat, a fishing vessel, six metres long, white. Please keep an eye open for this boat. Report any information you may have. Thank you. We're heading towards Saint-Mathieu. We'll take a look around there, towards the coast, unless you have any other news for us. Okay, great. I'll stay on on that frequency. I'll listen out for any more news and be in touch if I have any, of course. Do you have anything up in the air near the Courson point? No, we haven't seen anything yet. We're looking for a missing boat. It'd be nice to have some air support. Calling boat at position degrees, minutes north. Identify yourself, please, on channel 16. Over. The only call he seems to have made is to the repair yard to say he had a problem with his two engines. With no working engines, the boat is marooned. It could be floating helplessly in the shipping lanes, risking being crushed by a larger vessel. The picture of the missing boat is found on the Internet and the CROS uses it to provide more information. The boat must be found If the person on the boat has to spend the night at sea, he'll have some serious hypothermia problems the next morning. The temperatures drop to freezing out there. So unless he's got the right equipment on board, he's going to get really cold. Then a Coast Guard vessel reports that it's found the missing boat. We've found the boat. They'll sort it out on spot with him or are they going to turn back? Roger that. To tell him back. Each case becomes personal, really. It is a missing person, after all. So when they're located, it's still great news. Even after five or six years of doing this job and even after hundreds of such incidents, even if the person is safe and sound and never calls in to say, "Here I am, I'm okay," even then, we're happy. At the CROS, every call is a potential emergency, no matter how bizarre. A few weeks earlier, the center launched a search and rescue operation after an unusual phone call. Well, we got a call from someone in Paris who said, "I've just had a call from a Polish friend of mine in Poland, who says her husband has just called her to say his boat is sinking off the coast of Brittany." In fact, the story was absolutely true. As the pictures show, this man clinging to the wreck of his boat was in the middle of the busy sea lanes. By a miracle, he'd survived more than 10 hours in this dangerous waterway. Back home in Warsaw, Janusz Kapitosky, aged 68, admits he's lucky to be alive. A veteran of the Polish Navy, he was on around the world solo cruise when disaster struck off the coast of France. After six months without the slightest problem, just as he was entering the Straits of Ushant, his boat was struck by several huge waves. The first wave overturned the boat but it was okay. I was able to use the pump and emptied out the boat in about minutes. Then there was a huge wave that caught me by surprise. It knocked me down and crushing three ribs, and the next thing I found myself in the water. Despite the storm, I was able to swim back to the boat and climb back on board. The mast, unfortunately, had been broken, it was in three pieces. A few hours later, Janusz saw another ship on the horizon. He was sure he was about to be saved. In the distance, I saw a freighter heading towards me. Naturally, I fired off my distress flares so that he could see me. ship was getting closer, slowly heading my way, a couple of hundred yards, then 100. It kept getting closer, but then it sailed straight past me. I could see the crew on the bridge. They were talking and laughing, and there I was down in the water screaming at the top of my voice. I managed to send up a second flare. The whole sky was lit up red from the flare, but the ship just sailed on by. In fact, I was lucky it didn't run me over. Alone in the middle of the ocean, Janusz has one slim hope left, his cell phone. Luckily, it was still working, but I'd lost my phone list. The only number I knew off by heart was that of my wife. When I got the call from my husband, I started to cry straight away. He told me to stop crying and get a grip of myself. I decided, yes, he was right, it wouldn't help. Then I looked numbers. I found those for the rescue services, and I rang. Barbara doesn't speak French, so she rang a friend in Paris who in turn alerted the CROS. We ordered up a Navy Falcon 50 aircraft and it found a sailing ship with a broken mast. After 10 hours in the water, it was Janusz's first ray of hope. I noticed that the plane circled twice as in a rescue mission. It circled over me and then headed back to land. I was sure then at this point that it had spotted me and I was about to be saved because I was the only guy out there in the sea. The Falcon radios the closest ship and asks her to pick up Janusz, which is not an easy maneuver. The seas were quite strong at the time and they were looking just for a small boat. We had found it. We had its exact position and an infrared image from the rear navigator. At that point, we were concerned that the container vessel had not seen it or would not be able to maneuver in time because the sea was so rough. Despite the bad weather, the freighter was able to perform brilliantly. It was even more skillful since it had to sail past the sailing boat in order to align itself properly alongside so as to hoist the skipper up on board from his boat using rope ladder along its hull. You can see how small my yacht was in comparison to the freighter. I could see the man who was coming to help me up the ladder. I thought I'd never make it. It was a long climb up, believe me, and the sea was very choppy. The waves would lift me five or six metres and then take me right down again, even below water. The skipper's body temperature was degrees, and he was in a state of shock. So we radioed the CROS to let them know about his condition, and they decided right away to medevac him off. The first medical attention was given by Dr. Bombeir. He still remembers the moment well. When I finished my examination, I told the diver, "Okay, we can hoist him over." So we helped him up and explained what was going on. Then there were cheers from the ship's crew and the way the sailors and the Pole looked at each other, well, you knew that they knew a life had been saved that day. Even today, I can't really find the right words to express all my gratitude. They were real sailors, these people. It was their determination that saved my life. It was and still is a very emotional experience for me. It makes me cry just to think about it. Amateur sailors are not the only ones to be caught out by sudden bad weather. Trinite-Sur-Mer is the home port of Laurent Bourgnon, one of the best sailors in the world and twice the winner of the Route du Rhum Round the World Yacht Race. Bourgnon has been sailing in the waters around Brittany ever since he was young. He knows them well. Listen, the weather around here can really sneak up and surprise you. The sudden are tides, there's hidden rocks, things like that. As the saying goes, if you can navigate your way around Brittany, then you can just about sail anywhere It's one of the toughest areas in the world for sailors here. Bourgnon is tough. More than 20 years ago, he sailed single handed across the Atlantic in a hobby, a tiny catamaran with no equipment on board whatsoever. It bordered on lunacy. I encountered several storms on the way, huge walls of water that crashed down on top of me. Extremely violent, but there's not much you can do. Just pray, and that's when the fear might overwhelm you. A few years later, and his reputation as one of the best skippers in the world was established by winning several single handed races. A large part of the work is just concentrating on staying alive, to keep the boat on course in the right direction and to focus on what the vessel is doing and adjust depending on the elements. So if you stop concentrating, even for just a moment, things can go really badly. In 1996, he takes part in the British transatlantic race. On the fourth day, the rescue services receive a distress signal from Laurent Bourgnon. His trimaran has been overcome by a storm. There were heavy seas. We were just in front of a weather depression, and about hours later, a Filipino freighter, which was carrying iron ore, which was the ship nearest to me at the time, approached me, and in a quite remarkable manoeuvre towards the wind, managed to send me a large rope over which meant I could climb up onto the freighter. The crew was really fantastic. I had a great welcome. They treated me wonderfully. You see, because when you're out in the ocean, you're just a small thing there. So this kind of camaraderie is fantastic. When you're sailing single handed, things can go wrong. So it was great to get a helping hand like that. That's something you should never forget, but unfortunately, back in Paris, sometimes you do. Today, Laurent Bourgnon has stopped racing, but he still sails off the coast of Brittany. Okay. Take care, guys. He's never forgotten his rescue at sea. It's not just the professionals that act as guardian angels. In every port in Brittany, volunteers risk their lives to save those in peril at sea. These are the volunteers of the Aber Wrac'h in the Finistere. No storm ever stops these men from going out to sea. Oh, it's good for your health. At the wheel is [Ginou Letoirdec 01:14:46]. With him, Bakers, teachers, fishermen make up his crew. They are all volunteers. The guys with me here are volunteers. They want to be here, so they really try their best. Each man in this crew has saved the life of at least one sailor in peril. Their specialty seems to be rescues near the coast, helping those whose ships have run aground, as these library pictures make clear during a rescue operation. A few minutes later and there would have been no survivors. Back on land, Ginou works at the Port Authority. He agrees to be filmed, but insists that he is no hero. A rescue mission is just to help the others. It might be us one day. It could be your brother, or one day it might even be our son. It doesn't matter who it is or that we know them or not. I believe all sailors around the world would go to the other's assistance. It's a tradition that unfortunately, though, is disappearing, no sailor would want to leave another sailor- excuse me- in the shit. Despite their courage, the volunteer rescue services sometimes can only do so much. Once quite by chance, we came across a canoe that had been turned over, and amongst those who had disappeared was a child. His father was still alive. We warmed up the survivors. We took some of them home and then went looking for the boy and the others. The next day they were found. It was a ship from Saint-Malo that actually picked them up. It wasn't a good experience. It brought tears to our eyes, actually, knowing that the father had held his boy's hand for 7 hours and that he'd finally had to let go. Just thinking of that and knowing how the father must have felt, thinking, "I've lost my son." It must have been terrible. In churches throughout Brittany, there are memorials to those lost at sea. The rescuers remain modest, though. The sea is dominant because it's far stronger than we are. So occasionally we do get the worst of it. If we try to get smart, it will tell us, "Be careful because I know that I will win." When sea is calm, Ginou spends time fixing up his boat. He's been doing this for more than years. I don't think we're playing with death. No, I've never really felt scared. Worried, of course. Shaking hands, wobbly legs, it's just anxiety, though. You have to overcome it. It's just in your head. That's where panic sets in, and the more you panic, the more trouble you'll find yourself in, and then you freeze up totally. I've never really been scared when I'm in the thick of it. It's afterwards that you start thinking about it, and I think, "Well, that was close, too close maybe." In 1986, some of the volunteers paid with their lives. We lost five friends. We never really knew what happened to them. They left one night to go and help a sailing boat that had asked for assistance. The weather was bad, the weather was really bad, five or six-metre-high waves. It was a bit like this morning. They then lost power on board, a blackout, which means that you're in heavy seas like that, you've lost your radar, you have nothing. Five crew, five dead. Every time Ginou leaves port, he thinks of his five friends, volunteers like him who died trying to save others. Whenever I go past here, it's like visiting the grave of a close family member. There was a strong current and heavy winds and they were swept there onto the rocks in just a few seconds, despite attempts to anchor the ship. Because later they found the chain and the anchor, it had been not strong enough to stop them. They even tried to put their life raft out to sea, but it was too late. The ship was tossed between those two rocks there. It turned over and just broke up. We found the wreck over there behind that. You always think of them when you go past here? Of course, yes, always. This winter, off the coast of Brittany, the rescue teams were called out more than 1,500 times. They saved dozens of lives, although the sea claimed some too, and there were no ecological disasters along the coastline.

And then when you get there, there's going to be beach bars, restaurants, maybe a waterfall hike, or some other natural attraction. In the Mediterranean. You're typically docking each night. So you can walk off and on the boat as you please. There's going to be cobblestone roads, shops to visit. Maybe some fortresses or ruins, it's usually a bit more of a cultural experience and a lot to take in. So, that's how the overview of what it's like to be on a yacht charter for a week. There's obviously a little bit more that goes into it, like picking the right boat and destination, and that's where we come in and help provide some personalized recommendations. It's a bit of a conversation where we get to know you and what you're looking for, and then send you a variety of options that would meet your criteria and then help you evaluate them and narrow it down until you find the perfect yacht. That's it. That's how yacht charters work. In a nutshell. If you have any questions, please don't hesitate to reach out. We'd love to help you plan your next trip. And if you liked this video, give us a like, and follow us on YouTube for more tips on how to plan a charter. Thanks again. Bye.

- CHAPTER5 Networking in the Yacht World - Meet the right people: Kind: captions Language: en riding the wave mastering yacht interviews the yachting industry stands distinct not just because of its inherent luxury and charm but also due to its unique professional demands as such acing a yacht interview can be a bit different than conventional job interviews and preparing accordingly is crucial just like you wouldn't set sail without checking the weather you shouldn't dive into an interview without proper preparation this guide is here to ensure you ride the W with confidence and leave a lasting impression navigating different Waters common interview formats one face-to-face interviews this is the most traditional format and often preferred in the yachting industry as it allows for a more personal connection these can take place on the yacht at a yacht club or an office setting two over the phone while less personal phone interviews are not uncommon especially for preliminary discussions or when logistical challenges arise three video calls with technological advancements and the global nature of yachting video interviews via platforms like Zoom or Skype have become more prevalent they combine the convenience of phone interviews with the Personal Touch of face-to-face meetings charting the course typical interview questions and strategies to answer them one why are you interested in the yachting industry showcase genuine passion maybe it's the Allure of the Sea the intricacies of yachting operations or the chance to work with diverse clientele two what relevant experience do you bring to this position highlight experiences that showcase your technical abilities soft skills and adaptability don't just focus on tasks emphasize what you learned or how you added value three how do you handle challenging or high pressure situations narrate a real life instance where you faced a challenge how you addressed it and the outcome this not only provides proof of your problem solving skills but also demonstrates your ability to reflect and learn four have you ever had disagreements with crew members how did you handle it honesty is key if you did have disagreements describe them without casting blame focusing on the resolution and what you learned from the situation five what do you believe is the essence of excellent service on a luxury yacht dive into the intricacies of luxury service emphasizing attention to detail anticipating guest needs discretion and personalization six how do you stay updated with Maritime regulations safety protocols or any
