you are yachting in the Mediterranean or the Caribbean it's just a fact my starting salary as a junior deckhand with no experience was 4500 a month with a promise that every 6 months we would get a $500 raise so after a year it was 5,500 a month additionally chter tips are usually about 20% of the charter price of the yacht split evenly throughout the crew that means the deck hand and the stewards get the exact same share as the captain the chief stew the first mate to give an example I once did a 12-day charter in St Barts and everyone on the crew was tipped1 13,300 and we were all given a gift Midway through the chter I was literally told to run ashore and spend $3,500 as quickly as I could as a Christmas gift from the chter guest this was tiered so the chief stew and the first officer got way bigger gifts than me but it was still pretty awesome I got a Caribbean exclusive tag watch by the way and the chief officer got got a lot of Louis Vuitton it was freaking awesome so safe to say the pay is pretty good not to mention you have no living expenses if you're smart all of your food comes to you and it's like steak and lobster and you have no rent you have no car payment you can literally live for free and save 100% of your income if you wanted to many people can go into the yadaan industry travel and legitimately save away $100,000 in just one year so it doesn't really have to be a long-term career for you this is something that you could do for a year or two and really get yourself ahead in life okay is it like below deck this is a tough one because it's a yes and it's a no below deck isn't scripted it's very much real but it is edited they don't show you the 3 hours that I spent washing dishes every night because that would be boring the biggest difference I can say is that cursing at your boss getting in fights at work hooking up with crew eccentric guest it all exists in the yachting industry in real life but it's not exactly prevalent and it's certainly not tolerated for very long the first year that I worked on Excellence 6 I had 3 days off and the entire year and never hooked up with crew I saved all my money and I would always respond to my superiors which was everyone on the crew saying yes sir yes ma'am it would be my pleasure and if people ask me to jump I'd say how high that is what is expected in the yting industry some boats do allow the crew to party on the boat have fun it exist but some don't at all the guests are a little different too although some guests can be very demanding and eccentric on most boats I imagine that the reality TV aspect of below deck and the fact that you're being filmed on your vacation attracts a little bit different of a clientele and my experience with yachting on really big boats people pay that much money because they just want privacy and quiet so generally the guest behavior is a little different are tattoos allowed I think that I am living proof that you can work in the yachting industry with tattoos that being said I have had to wear sleeves to cover up my tattoos to work on some boats and some boats just won't hire we have a guest who's going to help me finish the rest of the video by the way but what I was saying is that clean cut classy look is really important to some chter guest this is a really high-end industry so you have to be honest with yourself if you've got a nose piercing a lot of ear piercings you need to take them out for interviews you probably need to take them off at the workplace and if you have visible tattoos it is going to limit you in some ways but if I can do it you can do it just be aware of this as you're looking forward to getting into the yacht industry it might not be the time to get that cute little pineapple on on your wrist just just hold off on it Landing the job okay so you're either in Fort Lauderdale on tbes or Palma and you've done all your training and it's time to land the job this is where the work comes in you simply need to get yourself out there network network network sign up with multiple crew agencies as the first thing this is a service that the boat actually pays for in exchange for qualified candidates from the crew agency so if you sign up for these crew agencies they're going to learn about who you are what type of job you're looking for remember you're looking for a busy Charter program and then they're going to help place you into some jobs that being said you can't rely on the crew agencies you also have to get out there yourself if the crew agency lands you an interview you're going to stay in the city so that you can do that interview in person and you're going to show up wearing a collared shirt your hair back clean as a whistle and a belt and shorts boat shoes looking the part you're going to be very respectful because manners go a long way in the charter industry there's also a lot of Facebook groups that post job listings in pretty much every location in the world for Charter Yachts some boats don't want to pay the fee to crew agencies a lot of the listings that you'll see on there post the position they talk about the experience needed any certificates that they're looking for they post the pay they talk about how big the crew size is they tell you the programs uh schedule so if nothing else it's good to peruse all these Facebook groups and figure out what's out there but join all the groups reply to all of them call the chief stes call the first officers and try to set interviews for yourself the next thing you're going to do is you're going to print out a stack of your CVS with copies of all of your certificates from your yacht training and business cards for your contact then you're going to walk down the dock and talk to yach crew and ask them if they're hiring doc talking is definitely the most intimidating part of the getting a job process but it's necessary and I promise you it works I have been working on the back of Yachts when crew came up presented their CV and I have seen people land a job this way so it's not BS you should do it just remember to be polite be friendly and hide your crazy because the yacht crew that you give your CV to are going to assess you and they're going to decide whether they want to work with you or live with you and they're the ones who decide if the captain gets your CV or if the trash can gets your CV finally go after day work you need to walk down the docks and look for boats that just need an extra hand for a day 2 days maybe a week you're going to get those jobs you're going to get paid a day rate which is often really good like a couple hundred a day and you're going to work your butt off and then immediately add that experience even if it's just one day on a tiny little center console boat you're going to add that experience to your CV and you're going to add the captain as a reference this is going to show chter boats who look at your CV and it's going to show the crew agency that you're serious about this you're actively working in the yacht industry this is what you do you work on boats and you work hard as far as the CV goes there's lots of online resources for how to create a perfect CV the format what to say on there and there's examples of other people's CVS so go through that spend a lot of time on it because trust me people are going to look at it in depth also don't worry about not having any experience a lot of big boats actually prefer to hire unexperienced green crew because it means that they could mold them the way that they want to one of the worst things to hear on a boat is one of your co-workers says that's not how we did it on our last boat so embrace the fact that you're green and say on your CV that you are a quick learner you respect your authorities oh also that you take Direction well that's a good one some other Sound Advice that I don't hear a lot of people giving is be picky when you get that first job offer you're going to want to take it but just remember there's lots of boats in the sea and you're interviewing them too this isn't like most jobs it might not be like any job you've ever worked on with this job you live with your co-workers you are literally with your captain and your crew 24/7 so if somebody doesn't get along with you it's going to ruin the experience for you wait for a job where you get along with the crew and you like the pay because I promise they're out there I'm going to give you a bonus tip and that is save your money yachting is notorious for lifestyle creep see when you're working with billionaires and Millionaires and you see this lavish lifestyle of designer handbags and rxes and Beach Clubs it happens to so many people in the yachting industry that you get that big fat Charter tip and you've got that cash in your pocket and you're somewhere amazing and you spend all of it don't let that happen to you stay on the boat go for dollar beers eat the crew food save your money because no matter how optimistic you are the honest truth is that yachting isn't a long-term career for most people a lot of people go all the way they become Chief stews they become psers first officers captains and you can make a lifelong career of this but what is most likely is that it's just a couple years long chapter for you so make sure that when you walk away from it if you walk away from it you can because you have a big old savings account full of that money I hope that was helpful for you and I hope that you feel encouraged to go get a yacht job because - A Day in the Life of a $100 Million Super Yacht Crew | Luxury Yacht Lifestyle: Kind: captions Language: en Luxury super yachts are floating palaces, complete with swimming pools, helipads, Michelin star chefs, and sometimes even submarines. But behind the glamour is a hidden workforce that keeps everything running like clockwork. Today, we're pulling back the curtain to explore a day in the life of a crew working aboard a hund00 million super yacht. From dawn until midnight, where every second is about serving the ultra rich. The early morning, the crew's day starts before the guests even open their eyes. At 5:00 a.m., engineers run checks on the yacht's engines, power systems, and water supplies. By 6:00 a.m., stewards are polishing chrome railings, scrubbing decks, and setting up breakfast tables with precision. Every glass, plate, and napkin must be spotless. Even the sun lounges are perfectly aligned. Why? Because on a $100 million yacht, perfection is the bare minimum. Guest breakfast and morning routines. When guests wake up, the crew transforms into invisible hosts. Some guests want a quiet breakfast on the deck with ocean views. Others demand a five course meal, fresh pastries, exotic fruit flown in from another country, and coffee brewed to their exact taste. Meanwhile, the water sports team prepares jet skis, paddle boards, and diving equipment. Every guest request must be anticipated before it's even asked. Late morning activities, late mornings are often about entertainment. Guests might take a helicopter ride from the yacht's helipad, go deep sea fishing, or relax in the jacuzzi. Crew members are trained to stay invisible yet available within seconds. If a guest drops a glass of champagne, someone replaces it instantly. If a child wants to snorkel, the crew sets it up in minutes. Lunch and afternoon service. By early afternoon, the chefs and kitchen staff prepare a lunch that rivals the world's best restaurants. Think sushi crafted from freshly caught fish, pasta made from scratch, and desserts plated like works of art. While guests dine, the crew continues their routine, polishing glass, refreshing towels, keeping everything running seamlessly. Guests should never notice the work behind the magic. Afternoon adventures. Afternoons are when super yacht life comes alive. Guests might request diving trips, jet skiing races, or even beach picnics set up on a private island. For the crew, this means moving fast, transporting food, drinks, and supplies without a single delay. And yes, sometimes that includes setting up a full luxury dining experience on the sand, complete with champagne and caviar. The hidden crew world. But while the guests play, the hidden crew world never stops. Below deck, engineers keep the yacht systems running. Power, sewage, water desalination, and air conditioning. Stewards handle endless laundry, polishing, and maintenance. The engine room is the beating heart of the yacht, and one mistake could ruin the guest experience. Dinner and evening entertainment. As the sun sets, it's time for the main event, dinner. Guests often expect fine dining experiences, multicourse meals, paired wines, and live entertainment. Chefs may spend the entire day preparing a single dinner, while the interior crew transform the yacht into a glowing palace. After dinner, guests may party into the night. DJs, champagne towers, or movies projected on the yacht's upper deck. For the crew, the work never stops. They serve, clean, and anticipate needs until the very last guest goes to bed. By midnight, when the guests finally fall asleep, the crew's work isn't finished. They clean every area, reset every detail, and prepare for the next day. Working on a 100 millionoll yacht is glamorous, but it's also grueling. Long hours, constant pressure, and perfection expected at every moment. So the next time you see a super yacht cruising through the ocean, remember behind the glamour is a crew of hardworking people making it all possible. Would you take the job if given the

- Russia superyacht mystery: Who owns Alfa Nero? - BBC World Service Documentaries: Kind: captions Language: en Since April, the Alfa Nero has languished in Falmouth Harbour, seized by the Antiguan government who said the multi-million pound yacht had been abandoned. This is the swimming pool helipad. Its helipad unused, the retractable swimming pool covered up. Its valuable artwork seen only by the crew. What is known about the person that originally owned this? No, I don't know absolutely nothing about this one. Because I have to take care of the boat from the port authority. It may be stranded, but the Alfa Nero is not forgotten. This yacht is at the centre of an international legal battle that has revealed for the first time how one of Russia's richest men has managed his wealth in the West and how a British company has helped him. There is a huge amount of sensitivity around and interest in this boat. We came out here being told that we could film the whole thing but in the end they've only let us on a small bit. It was the decision of the captain. Now why was that? It's not because of the boat itself, but because of sensitivity around the man who allegedly owned it. This is Andrey Guryev, a sanctioned Russian billionaire and the man the US government says owned the Alfa Nero. For one of Russia's richest men and a former senator, relatively little is known about him. Mr Guryev was among a group of businessmen who took advantage of the collapse of the Soviet Union to acquire former state assets and make a vast fortune. His was in the fertiliser industry, setting up a company called PhosAgro. Mining phosphate rock for fertiliser proved hugely lucrative, propelling the company onto the London Stock Exchange. Igor Sychev once headed the company's tax department, but he fell out with Mr Guryev over a stake at the firm. Tell me about Mr Guryev. What's he like? He's a controversial person. He truly believes in God publicly and as it turned out, he loves luxury. The US government describes Mr Guryev as a known close associate of Vladimir Putin, one that has been living the high life. In 2008, Mr Guryev bought Wittenhurst House in North London. It's behind those rather imposing looking gates. This is as close as we can get. He took what was a dilapidated Georgian mansion and turned it into one of London's most expensive properties with a huge number of rooms, a swimming pool, a vast underground basement and parking for 25 cars. Like much of Moscow's oligarch elites, he was enjoying a quiet life here in London and then the invasion of Ukraine started. A missile strike on an airport As the news reverberates around the world, Vladimir Putin assembles the oligarchs Thirty-seven members of Russia's business elite that control key sectors of the economy. One of those in attendance is Andrey Guryev's son. Like many of the men in this room, he'll be sanctioned by the West along with his father, but not quite yet. In the Caribbean, the Alfa Nero turns its tracker off. The ship goes dark. As other oligarch-linked yachts scramble to get out of the reach of sanctions, the first big bust happens in northern Italy where a number are seized. This is one of the yachts that has been taken by European authorities. A second yacht, linked to Mr Guryev, leaves port in Livorno. Recently built, Luminosity is one of the biggest hybrid superyachts in the world, catering for 27 guests and 37 crew on its six decks. It makes its way around the boot of Italy and into non-European Union Montenegro, where it's safe from EU sanctions for now. As pressure mounts on more countries to condemn Russia's invasion, Montenegro's government ignores significant pro-Russian sentiment and adopts EU sanctions. A month after Luminosity arrives in Montenegro, an inspector boards and finds that it's owned by a company registered in Guernsey. Here a pattern emerges. The companies that own the palatial Wittenhurst estate and Alfa Nero are linked to the same address as Luminosity. And that address is here on the British Crown Dependency of Guernsey. It's a place known for beautiful beaches, a relaxed pace of life, and of course, offshore finance. That is the office of Opus Private. Now on their website, they said they help some of the world's wealthiest individuals to manage and protect their assets. Now normally that's being done under a veil of offshore secrecy, something that's rather harder with Russia's invasion of Ukraine. This company's house filing from May is for one of the Guryev family's UK mansions that we've traced back to Opus. In all, we've found half a billion pounds worth of assets and a relationship going back at least a decade. It is legal to continue to work for a sanctioned client with government permission, and court documents we've obtained from Antigua show Opus was involved in a failed legal action to stop the Alfa Nero being auctioned off. The judgement details how the ownership of the superyacht is tied up in a trust, which a judge in Antigua suggests Mr Guryev still exercises a degree of control over. In the latest challenge to the yacht's seizure, Mr Guryev's daughter is trying to argue that she is the sole beneficiary of the trust which owns Alfa Nero. When you are a very wealthy individual, you want to protect your wealth. So obviously you would trust your own relatives to take care of their wealth should something happen to you. And is that process completely legal? It is legal as long as the individual is not sanctioned. If the individual is later sanctioned and then decides to restructure their own wealth, that's when it's not legal and that becomes sanction evasion. When it comes to these Guryev family assets, including Alfa Nero, there's no way of us independently finding out when Mr Guryev's daughter became the sole beneficiary. Both Opus and Mr Guryev's daughter told us it happened before her father was sanctioned. Opus also insisted that Mr Guryev is not their client, as he is no longer a beneficiary of the trust, despite being connected to half a billion pounds worth of the family's assets. Knowing who really owns assets is essential if sanctions are to be effective. Complex ownership arrangements like the ones we've found make that much harder. This kind of cat and mouse game of using multi-jurisdictional approaches to try and hide what you're doing is a real challenge. And actually that's where diplomacy comes in, where the foreign office should be saying we expect more from you. The Guryevs both resigned their positions at PhosAgro, but despite the sanctions, the family remain major shareholders. The company has made record sales since the invasion of Ukraine. Guriyev Jr even met President Putin to discuss the fertiliser industry. It's led to calls from Kiev for the UK to go further. Vlad Vlasiuk is President Zelensky's sanctions advisor. He's overseen not just the freezing, but the seizing of assets, something their allies have largely failed to do. We have this aggression unleashed at us and we just have to protect ourselves with all the means available and we've been doing quite intensively this confiscation of the assets belonging to the Russian individuals. In all, they've sanctioned around 9,000 people and companies, including Mr Guryev and his son, but also his wife and daughter. Mr Guryev being one of the biggest players in the field of fertilisers, Mr Putin himself has been weaponising the fertiliser and causing a lot of threat to the whole of the world in terms of global food security and we just don't want that to happen. The UK government says sanctioned assets, like the Guriyev families, will remain frozen until Russia pays reparations. It's clear Russia must pay for the destruction that they've inflicted. So we're working with allies to explore lawful routes to use Russian assets. What would you like to see happen to the UK-based assets of Mr Guryev? I think that those assets should be confiscated and then transferred to Ukraine. The money would be very much used to rebuild Ukraine and that would also send a strong message A spokesman for Mr Guryev said there was no proper or legitimate basis for the sanctions against him. Opus Private told us trusts are legal structures and their use is lawful. Opus provides trust services to and for the benefit of the discretionary beneficiaries of the trust structure. It provides those trust services in compliance with all applicable laws. And that Opus has made the Guernsey and UK authorities aware of its ownership and control of trust assets, which it treats as frozen, in line with the UK sanctions against Mr Guryev. Despite being auctioned off, the Alfa Nero remains in Falmouth Harbour. Its fate disputed in the courts. It is a powerful symbol of how a sanctioned Russian oligarch can claim, with lawful British help, that the yacht and his family's other assets in the West are not really his at all.

Francis' pride and joy, the Marie-Joseph, left Poland more than two months ago. The catamaran is due to arrive in a few minutes along these docks. For Francis, finally, the long awaited moment. His solar-powered boat makes a spectacular entrance into the marina. How do you feel? I’m happy. It’s a brand-new technology. There's the wardrobe there. -Do you like the colors? -The colors are nice. Yes, of course. I didn't think it would move me that much. There is emotion. I've always taken it lightly, but now that it's for real, it's still… Before casting off, Francis awaits the arrival of a distinguished guest. It’s Sheikh Racher Al Maktoum, the dean of Dubai. Congratulations for this beautiful girl. Once everyone is on board, the five crew members are busy. It's time to set sail. A yacht of the future. The Marie-Joseph traces its wake, and with it, a new page in nautical history is written. At the helm, no stress. The two electric motors meet all of Francis' expectations. Now see how much the wake is at the back. Its maximum speed is 15 knots. It's actually quite impressive. It's just electric engines. It's very easy to operate the boat. He doesn't need to do power management like a lot of boats where you need to check what uses how much, and you cannot have the fridge and the dishwasher and the cooking on. This is just energy management because we have way too much power. If it's empty, he'll start the generator or hope for the sun. -Easy life? -Easy life, exactly. How much is on the battery? On the battery, we have 23, 24 degrees. We did them in Gdansk but it was a bit chilly. Then we didn't do them for long, so it works well. -It works well. -Your first impressions? It's great, it works well, and we’re happy. It's just like we expected. Even some details are better than we expected. We have a better speed. It's nice when you hear nothing, no vibration, nothing. -The feel is different. -Now they come next to you. -You see what this is. You'll be popular now. -This new technology is impressive. What do you think? Yes, everyone will talk about it. As I told you, this is the first one to come to UAE. The first solar sailboat. An 80-feet, not a small one. As Francis had hoped, the Marie-Joseph is a sensation. A proud ship that sails without a drop of fuel and in complete silence. That's what we appreciate, luxury in the end. It's not just a boat. The great luxury is the silence, no vibrations, nothing. We're not going backwards anymore. You don't buy a boat with diesel anymore, that’s clear. There is no turning back. The yachting world must evolve. To rectify the disastrous impact on ecology, the electric engine is only one of the possible alternatives. The next great promise for the sector that Francis Lapp and other manufacturers are working on is hydrogen. A new revolution that is already underway.

  1. @kemusikemusi: グレタは、なぜこの船に乗り込まないのですか？
  2. @DisciplinePelajar: Monaco, Billionaire Playground 😎
  3. @high-reshallucinations: 1. Russian oligarch yachts
After the Ukraine war sanctions, many Russian-owned yachts were seized in places like Spain, Italy, France, and the US. Some owners moved their yachts to Turkey because Turkey did not join Western sanctions.

2. Superyachts are symbols of extreme wealth
The film shows huge yachts with luxury cabins, crews, security, submarines, private events, and weekly rental prices around €500,000.

3. The environmental criticism
A major theme is that superyachts burn massive amounts of diesel. The transcript says some consume around 500 liters per hour, making them controversial because of their carbon footprint.

4. Life behind the luxury
The documentary contrasts guest luxury with crew life. Guests have huge suites, cinemas, decks, and private lounges, while crew members live in small shared cabins and work under strict standards.

5. New luxury markets
Saudi Arabia, Dubai, Hong Kong, and Thailand are shown as growing yacht hubs. Saudi Arabia wants places like Jeddah to become major luxury boating destinations.

6. “Green” yachts
A big part focuses on Francis Lapp and Sunreef in Poland, building solar/electric catamarans. Their yacht Marie-Joseph uses solar panels integrated into the hull and electric motors, aiming to reduce diesel use.

7. The contradiction
The documentary’s title suggests “Russian oligarch yachts,” but much of it is actually about the wider yacht industry: luxury, sales, chartering, crew life, Saudi/Dubai markets, and eco-yacht technology.

Overall: it’s less a pure investigation into Russian oligarchs and more a documentary about how the superyacht industry is trying to survive bad publicity by moving into new markets and promoting “eco-luxury.”

Which exactly matches the boat's product. Like their same design, their same model, their same features. Because all this is designed in China. Manufacturing takes place in China. Assembled in China. And then it is supplied to different companies in different countries. In which there is difference of only one thing and that is of people. Then the same product comes to India and its price becomes ₹2 to ₹3000 whereas the same product is made in China for ₹2 to ₹200. know why? Whereas on the contrary, do you know how much was spent on RAD ? Only ₹ crore. This means that the company is spending only ₹30 crore to make and design the product. And it is spending ₹800 crores to sell that product. From which it becomes clear how big a game was being played here. The result of which was that in 2023 the company delivered a loss of Rs 129 crore and in 2024 the same loss became Rs 53 crore. However, the company's revenue has also been stuck at around the same level for the last two-three years and that is around Rs 3000 crore and what is even more shocking is that they have not yet been successful in bringing their IPO. For which he has applied two to three times so far. Despite this, it is said that we are India's number one variables company which is looking like a marketing company in real life in today's time. Because their focus is more on selling the product through their marketing rather than making it.
